Claude finished the response

You said: Hey Claude, so I have students who are making a habit of coming late to class and the school system doesn't penalise them for it so in a way they are incentivi…

Hey Claude, so I have students who are making a habit of coming late to class and the school system doesn't penalise them for it so in a way they are incentivised to not arrive on time. I need to put an end to this. So I'm thinking I want to make a standard late form that they need to fill out which gives me all the pertinent information such as why they were late, did they check in at the front office, etc so that I then use that for the basis of a compass post which I will send straight home to parents.

09:09

Claude responded: Here's a standard late form you can use.

Here's a standard late form you can use.

Late Arrival Form

Student name: _______________ Year level/Class: _______________

Date: ____________ Time arrived in class: ____________ Period/Lesson: ____________

Did you check in at the front office? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If yes, late pass number/reference: _______________

How many minutes of class did you miss? _______________

Reason for being late:

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Yes ☐ No

Have you been late to this class before this week? ☐ Yes ☐ No — If yes, how many times: ____

What will you do differently to arrive on time next lesson?

Student signature: _______________

For teacher use:
Work/notes missed: _______________
Compass entry logged: ☐ Parent notified: ☐ Follow-up required: ☐

A few things worth considering for it to actually stick:

The "what will you do differently" question does quiet work — it forces a moment of accountability and gives you a quotable line for the Compass post. The "have you been late before this week" field lets you escalate proportionately rather than treating a one-off the same as a pattern.

Want me to draft a matching Compass post template (with a neutral, factual tone that holds up if a parent pushes back), or turn the form into a clean printable Word doc?

You said: yes I know the restorative justice framework so i understand your questioning, i want this to be even longer though.

yes I know the restorative justice framework so i understand your questioning, i want this to be even longer though. I want the form to actually be a pain in the arse to fill out

09:10

Claude responded: Got it — the form itself as the deterrent.

Got it — the form itself as the deterrent. The friction is the point: if filling it out costs more than arriving on time, the incentive flips. Here's a much longer version.

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Front Office Check-In

Did you check in at the front office before coming to class? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If yes, what time did you check in? ______
Late pass / reference number issued: ______
If you did NOT check in, explain why not:

Section C — Reason (be specific)

Where were you in the period between when school started / the previous lesson ended and when you arrived here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late?

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer to the above:

Section D — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

How does one student arriving late affect the rest of the class when the teacher has to stop and settle them in? Write at least two sentences.

Section E — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______
If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

Section F — Plan

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time?

What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

Section G — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Repeat offender — escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

A couple of honest flags, since you value the straight version:

The length does the deterrent work, but the impact/reflection questions (Sections D and Pattern) are also what make it defensible if a parent challenges you — it's clearly framed as restorative, not punitive, even though the friction is deliberate. That framing protects you.

One risk: a genuinely-late-for-a-real-reason kid (missed bus, family issue) gets the same wall of admin as a serial offender. The "within your control" and pattern fields let you mentally triage, but you might want a line at the top like "If you were late due to a medical or family emergency, see the teacher before completing this form" so the blunt instrument doesn't catch the wrong kid and hand a parent a legitimate grievance.

Want this as a printable Word doc, plus the matching Compass post template?

You said: just keep in mind they may not be late to school, but late to my class.

just keep in mind they may not be late to school, but late to my class. keep that in mind. there should be a section where they get a signature from prior teacher if he or she kept them late.

09:12

Claude responded: Good catch — that changes the framing.

Good catch — that changes the framing. Many of these will be intra-school lateness, so the question isn't "where were you between home and school" but "what happened between the last lesson and mine." Here's the revised version with a teacher-signature section for legitimate hold-ups.

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.
If you were late due to a medical or family emergency, see the teacher before completing this form.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Where were you coming from?

Was this your first lesson/period of the day? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If NO, what class were you in immediately before this one? ______
Teacher of that class: ______
What time were you dismissed from that class? ______

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. A form without a signature here will be treated as not kept back.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

If you cannot get this signature, you must explain why:

Section D — Front Office Check-In

Did you need to check in at the front office (e.g. arrived late to school)? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If you should have checked in but did not, explain why:

Section E — Reason (be specific)

Account for your time. Where were you between being dismissed from your previous location and arriving here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late? ______

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer:

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

How does one student arriving late affect the rest of the class when the teacher has to stop and settle them in? Write at least two sentences.

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______
If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

Section H — Plan

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______
What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

Section I — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
Kept back by prior teacher (signature obtained) ☐ Genuine reason ☐ No valid reason ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

The teacher-signature section does something useful beyond fairness: it kills the most common excuse instantly. "Mr X kept me back" is unfalsifiable until you require the signature — now the kid either has proof (and you don't send the Compass post) or they don't (and the excuse evaporates). It also means you're never the one chasing down a colleague to verify; the burden sits with the student.

Want it as a printable Word doc now, plus the Compass post template for the ones who come back with no valid reason?

You said: no, the medical or family emergency line, why did you add that?

no, the medical or family emergency line, why did you add that? no, I. Am. Teaching. At. That. Time. I do not want to be disturbed.

09:13

Claude responded: Right — removing it.

Right — removing it. You can't be triaging emergencies at the classroom door mid-lesson; that defeats the whole point of the form absorbing the friction instead of you. The form does the work, you keep teaching. Anything genuine surfaces in the reason section and you deal with it after, via the Compass review.

Here's the form with that line gone.

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Where were you coming from?

Was this your first lesson/period of the day? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If NO, what class were you in immediately before this one? ______
Teacher of that class: ______
What time were you dismissed from that class? ______

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. A form without a signature here will be treated as not kept back.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

If you cannot get this signature, explain why:

Section D — Front Office Check-In

Did you need to check in at the front office (e.g. arrived late to school)? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If you should have checked in but did not, explain why:

Section E — Reason (be specific)

Account for your time. Where were you between being dismissed from your previous location and arriving here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late? ______

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer:

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

How does one student arriving late affect the rest of the class when the teacher has to stop and settle them in? Write at least two sentences.

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______
If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

Section H — Plan

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______
What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

Section I — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
Kept back by prior teacher (signature obtained) ☐ Genuine reason ☐ No valid reason ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

I also added "Do not interrupt the teacher" to the top instruction line, which reinforces the same boundary — they pick up the form, fill it in, sit down, and you review the stack at a break.

Want the printable Word doc and the Compass post template now?

You said: Section F impact question 3, should be contingent on whether they are genuinely late for a decent reason.

Section F impact question 3, should be contingent on whether they are genuinely late for a decent reason.

09:15

Claude responded: Right — if a kid was legitimately kept back by another teacher, making them write two sentences on how their lateness disrupts the class is unfair and undercut…

Right — if a kid was legitimately kept back by another teacher, making them write two sentences on how their lateness disrupts the class is unfair and undercuts the form's credibility. The impact reflection should only apply to the ones who are late through their own doing. Here's the fix — I've gated question 3 with a routing instruction.

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Only answer the question below if your lateness was within your control (i.e. you were NOT kept back by a teacher or delayed by a genuine reason beyond your control). If it was outside your control, write "N/A" and move on.

How does one student arriving late affect the rest of the class when the teacher has to stop and settle them in? Write at least two sentences.

That keeps the reflective/punitive weight on the students who earned it, while the genuinely-delayed kid just skips it. It also ties back cleanly to Section C (teacher signature) and Section E ("within your control") — the form is internally consistent now, so a student can't be marked down on impact while simultaneously holding a signature that proves they were held up.

Want the Word doc and Compass template now, or any other sections to adjust first?

show me the full draft again

09:16

Claude responded: Right — that line is a loophole.

Right — that line is a loophole. It invites a written excuse to stand in for the signature, which is exactly the unfalsifiable "Mr X kept me back" you're trying to kill. No signature means not kept back, full stop. Here's Section C cleaned up.

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. No signature means you were not kept back, and this section will not be accepted as a reason.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

That's the only change. The rest of the form stands as it was.

Want the printable Word doc and the Compass post template now?

You said: if you should have checked in but did not, explain why is janky english.

if you should have checked in but did not, explain why is janky english. Yes, they should check in. always.

09:17

Claude responded: Right — checking in isn't conditional, so the "if you should have" hedge is both clumsy and sends the wrong message.

Right — checking in isn't conditional, so the "if you should have" hedge is both clumsy and sends the wrong message. Here's Section D rewritten to state the expectation plainly.

Section D — Front Office Check-In

If you arrived late to school, you must check in at the front office. This is not optional.

Did you check in at the front office? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A (was not late to school)
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If no, state why you failed to check in:

That keeps the expectation absolute, the English clean, and still gives you the N/A path for kids who were only late to your class and didn't come through the office.

Want the Word doc and Compass template now?

You said: this one:

this one:

How does one student arriving late affect the rest of the class when the teacher has to stop and settle them in? Write at least two sentences.

Should be replaced by:

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

09:19

Claude responded: Done — swapped the single question for your two, keeping the same conditional gate above them.

Done — swapped the single question for your two, keeping the same conditional gate above them.

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Only answer the questions below if your lateness was within your control (i.e. you were NOT kept back by a teacher or delayed by a genuine reason beyond your control). If it was outside your control, write "N/A" and move on.

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

The two-question split is better than what I had — it makes them reckon with two distinct parties (peers and you) rather than one fuzzy "the class," which is harder to dash off and lands the accountability more squarely.

Want the full draft shown again, or straight to the Word doc and Compass template?

Claude responded: Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Where were you coming from?

Was this your first lesson/period of the day? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If NO, what class were you in immediately before this one? ______
Teacher of that class: ______
What time were you dismissed from that class? ______

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. No signature means you were not kept back, and this section will not be accepted as a reason.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

Section D — Front Office Check-In

If you arrived late to school, you must check in at the front office. This is not optional.

Did you check in at the front office? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A (was not late to school)
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If no, state why you failed to check in:

Section E — Reason (be specific)

Account for your time. Where were you between being dismissed from your previous location and arriving here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late? ______

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer:

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Only answer the questions below if your lateness was within your control (i.e. you were NOT kept back by a teacher or delayed by a genuine reason beyond your control). If it was outside your control, write "N/A" and move on.

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______
If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

Section H — Plan

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______
What specifically went wrong with that today?

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

For teacher use only:

Notes: _______________

You said: so qustions such as 'List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:'

so qustions such as 'List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:'

Should be in a section D or whatever that is only for students we have determined are not late for legitimate groundss

09:21

Claude responded: Good structural call — right now the reflective/accountability questions are scattered across sections that everyone fills out, including the legitimately-dela…

Good structural call — right now the reflective/accountability questions are scattered across sections that everyone fills out, including the legitimately-delayed kid. Better to ring-fence all the "you-earned-this" reflection into one block that's explicitly gated, so the kept-back student stops after the factual sections and the genuine offender carries on into the reflection.

Here's how I'd restructure: keep Sections A–E as the universal factual record (everyone completes), then collapse the reflective material (current Impact reflection questions + Plan) into a single gated section. Pattern can stay universal since it's just factual counts.

Here's the reworked back half:

Section F — Impact (factual — everyone completes)

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Section G — Pattern (everyone completes)

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If your lateness was outside your control — you were kept back by a teacher (and have their signature in Section C) or had a genuine reason beyond your control — you are finished. Hand the form in.

If your lateness was within your control, you must complete Section H below.

Section H — Accountability (only if your lateness was within your control)

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______
What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

This gives you a clean two-tier structure: the legitimately-delayed student does the factual record and stops; the genuine offender hits the wall of reflection. The visual "STOP" divider also makes the gate obvious rather than relying on them reading a parenthetical.

Want me to show the full redrafted form with this structure, then the Word doc?

You said: so show me the whole thing

so show me the whole thing

09:22

Claude responded: Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Where were you coming from?

Was this your first lesson/period of the day? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If NO, what class were you in immediately before this one? ______
Teacher of that class: ______
What time were you dismissed from that class? ______

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. No signature means you were not kept back, and this section will not be accepted as a reason.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

Section D — Front Office Check-In

If you arrived late to school, you must check in at the front office. This is not optional.

Did you check in at the front office? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A (was not late to school)
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If no, state why you failed to check in:

Section E — Reason (be specific)

Account for your time. Where were you between being dismissed from your previous location and arriving here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late? ______

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer:

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If your lateness was outside your control — you were kept back by a teacher (and have their signature in Section C) or had a genuine reason beyond your control — you are finished. Hand the form in.

If your lateness was within your control, you must complete Section H below.

Section H — Accountability (only if your lateness was within your control)

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______

What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

Section I — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
Kept back by prior teacher (signature obtained) ☐ Genuine reason ☐ No valid reason ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

Want this as a printable Word doc, plus the Compass post template?

You said: this section:

this section:

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______ How many times have you been late to ANY class this week? ______

Change to:

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______ How many times have you been late to absent from this class this week? ______

Were there different reasons for those late arrivals and absences? yes / no If so, what were they?______________________

Claude responded: Small wording note before I lock it in: "How many times have you been late to absent from this class" has a leftover "late to" in it.

Small wording note before I lock it in: "How many times have you been late to absent from this class" has a leftover "late to" in it. I'm reading your intent as wanting to capture absences from this class, so I've written it as "How many times have you been absent from this class this week?" — flag me if you meant something else.

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been absent from this class this week? ______

Claude responded: Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

Late Arrival Reflection & Accountability Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you join the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher. Incomplete forms will be returned to you to complete again.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Today's day of the week: ______
Scheduled lesson start time: ______
Actual time you entered the classroom: ______
Total minutes late (calculate and write the number): ______

Section B — Where were you coming from?

Was this your first lesson/period of the day? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If NO, what class were you in immediately before this one? ______
Teacher of that class: ______
What time were you dismissed from that class? ______

Section C — Were you kept back by a teacher?

Did a teacher keep you late, which caused your lateness to this class? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, you must get that teacher to sign below. No signature means you were not kept back, and this section will not be accepted as a reason.

I confirm I kept this student back until ______ (time), which contributed to their late arrival.
Prior teacher name: _______________ Signature: _______________

Section D — Front Office Check-In

If you arrived late to school, you must check in at the front office. This is not optional.

Did you check in at the front office? ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ N/A (was not late to school)
If yes, time checked in: ______ Late pass / reference number: ______
If no, state why you failed to check in:

Section E — Reason (be specific)

Account for your time. Where were you between being dismissed from your previous location and arriving here?

State the reason you were late:

What time did you realise you were going to be late? ______

Was this reason within your control? ☐ Fully ☐ Partly ☐ Not at all
Explain your answer:

Section F — Impact

What were the first instructions or content you missed by arriving late?

Who will you ask to find out what you missed (name a classmate, not the teacher)?

Section G — Pattern

How many times have you been late to THIS class in the past two weeks? ______
How many times have you been absent from this class this week? ______

Were there different reasons for those late arrivals and absences? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If so, what were they?

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If your lateness was outside your control — you were kept back by a teacher (and have their signature in Section C) or had a genuine reason beyond your control — you are finished. Hand the form in.

If your lateness was within your control, you must complete Section H below.

Section H — Accountability (only if your lateness was within your control)

How does your lateness affect the learning in class of other students?

How does your lateness affect the teacher of this class?

If this is a repeat, what did you say last time you would do differently?

What time do you need to leave your previous location to arrive on time? ______

What specifically went wrong with that today?

List two concrete things you will do differently next lesson:

Section I — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this form will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
Kept back by prior teacher (signature obtained) ☐ Genuine reason ☐ No valid reason ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

That's the polished full draft. Want it as a printable Word doc now, plus the Compass post template?

Claude responded: I'll build it as a Word doc.

I'll build it as a Word doc. Let me check the skill first.

Reading the docx skill before building the document

Now I'll build the document.

Ran 2 commands, created a file

Let me check how it renders.

Viewed 2 files, ran a command

The "STOP" divider lands well as a visual gate. Page 3 will hold Section I and the teacher box. It's rendering cleanly across three pages. The form is done.

Presented file

Here's the form as an editable Word doc. It runs to three pages, with the "STOP" divider acting as a clear visual gate between the universal factual sections (A–G) and the gated accountability section (H). The blue section rules and ruled writing lines make it print cleanly, and you can drop your school logo or letterhead in at the top if you want it on official stationery.

A couple of things you can tweak directly in the file: the "Teacher:" and "Class/Subject:" fields in Section A — if it's always your class, you could pre-fill those before printing a stack. Same with the school name.

Want the matching Compass post template now — the neutral, factual wording you send home for the ones who land in the "no valid reason" category?

Late arrival form
Document · DOCX 

You said: okay now i want to do another one for phone usage in the class.

okay now i want to do another one for phone usage in the class. Students will often refuse to put their phones in the phone caddy at the front of the class. Some may have genuine ignorance about phone rules in victorian schools so we can draw from that. So this would be a form that is given to students when they refuse to give up their phone or when they use their phone in class. A few ways that students use phones in class include: sneakily trying to photograph notes from the whiteboard to avoid writing, or even such as yesterday one student chose to receive a phone call in class. to research questions they have been asked (outsourcing problem solving and thinking)

09:50

Claude responded: Before I build this, let me check the current Victorian policy so the form's reference to the rules is accurate rather than something a student or parent could…

Before I build this, let me check the current Victorian policy so the form's reference to the rules is accurate rather than something a student or parent could pick apart.

Searched the web

Good — the policy picture is clear and current. The key facts for the form: Victorian government schools have banned phones since 2020; the rule is phones switched off and stored securely during the school day, not just "not used." That's the line that kills the "but I wasn't even using it" defence — storage is the requirement, so refusing the caddy is itself the breach. Teachers can grant a classroom-based learning exception, which matters: it means you are the authority on whether a phone comes out for a task, not the student. Victorian GovernmentVictorian Government

Before I draft, two structural questions so the form fits how you actually want to use it:

You said: Q: This form gets handed over in two quite different situations (refusing the caddy vs.

Q: This form gets handed over in two quite different situations (refusing the caddy vs. caught using the phone). How do you want it structured? A: One form covering both, with a tickbox for which happened

Q: How hard should the 'genuine ignorance of the rules' angle lean? A: Educative first — explain the rule, then accountability

09:51

Claude responded: Good — one form, educative-first.

Good — one form, educative-first. Here's the draft. The structure mirrors the late form so they feel like a coherent set: factual record up top, a rule-explainer box that does the "genuine ignorance" work, then the gated accountability section for students who knew and chose to breach anyway.

Mobile Phone & Device Incident Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you continue with the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher. Your phone or device stays with the teacher (or in the caddy) until the end of the lesson.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Time of incident: ______

Section B — What happened? (teacher will tick before handing you this form)

☐ Refused to place phone/device in the phone caddy when asked
☐ Used a phone/device in class without permission
☐ Took, or attempted to take, a photo/video/recording (e.g. of the whiteboard or notes)
☐ Made or received a phone call
☐ Used a phone/device to look up an answer instead of thinking through the task
☐ Wore or used a smart watch / earbuds / other device
☐ Other: _______________

Was a teacher's instruction to put the device away given before this form? ☐ Yes ☐ No
How many times were you asked? ______

Section C — Know the rule

Read this carefully. You are expected to understand it after today.

In Victorian government schools, mobile phones and personal devices must be switched off and stored securely for the whole school day — not just "not used." This rule has applied across the state since 2020. From the start of 2027 it is law in every Victorian school, and it also covers smart watches and earbuds.

This means:

  • Refusing to put your phone in the caddy is itself a breach, even if you weren't using it.
  • A phone only comes out for a task if the teacher has given the class permission for that specific activity. You do not decide this — the teacher does.
  • Using a phone to photograph notes, take a call, or look up an answer is not allowed.

After reading the above:
Did you already know this was the rule before today? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If you answered No, what did you previously believe the rule was?

Section D — Your account

In your own words, describe what you did with the device:

Why did you have the device out / why did you refuse to hand it over?

Did the teacher give the class permission to use phones for this task? ☐ Yes ☐ No

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If you have now read Section C and understand the rule, and this was a genuine first-time misunderstanding, tell the teacher — you are finished here. Hand the form in.

If you already knew the rule and chose to breach it, or you refused a direct instruction to hand the device over, you must complete Section E below.

Section E — Accountability (only if you knew the rule and chose to breach it, or refused an instruction)

If you used the phone to photograph notes or look up an answer: what skill or thinking were you avoiding by doing that, instead of doing it yourself?

How does a phone going off, a call, or a device being used affect the learning of other students in the room?

When a teacher gives a direct instruction and a student refuses, what does that do to the lesson?

What will you do with your phone at the start of the next lesson, and every lesson after?

Section F — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this incident will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass. I understand that repeated or serious breaches may be escalated.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
First-time misunderstanding ☐ Knew rule, chose to breach ☐ Refused direct instruction ☐
Device confiscated until end of lesson ☐ Returned to student ☐ Held at office ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

A few notes on the deliberate moves:

The Section C → "did you already know?" sequence is the trap that makes the educative angle work for you rather than against you. A genuinely ignorant kid reads the rule, ticks "No," and is routed out — fair, and it can't be weaponised against you. But a repeat offender can't claim ignorance, because the form is dated and recorded; the second time they fill one in, "No, I didn't know" is no longer available to them. The educative framing quietly builds your evidence trail.

The "received a phone call" and "looked up an answer instead of thinking" items are called out specifically in Section B and E because they're the behaviours you flagged — naming them on the form signals to the student (and any parent) that these are recognised, repeated problems, not you improvising a punishment.

One thing worth your judgement: the "what skill were you avoiding" question in Section E is sharp — it's pedagogically pointed at the outsourcing-of-thinking problem, which is your real concern with the research-on-phone behaviour. But it assumes a level of metacognitive honesty some kids won't give you. If you find it draws blank stares or smart-arse answers, tell me and I'll swap it for something more concrete.

Want me to build this into a matching Word doc in the same style as the late form?

You said: nope.

nope. Your opener doesn't work because if they refuse to give me their phone then we do not reach section A. you need to get them to write a response to 'did you refuse to hand your phone to the teacher or place in the caddy? why did you refuse to do this? explain your reason.

09:52

Claude responded: Right — if the phone's still in their hand, the form can't be premised on the device already being surrendered.

Right — if the phone's still in their hand, the form can't be premised on the device already being surrendered. The refusal is the live situation the form has to address head-on, not a tickbox after the fact. Here's the reworked opener and Section B.

I'd change the top instruction line to drop the "stays with the teacher" assumption, and make the refusal the first substantive thing they answer:

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you continue with the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Time of incident: ______

Section B — Did you refuse to hand over your phone?

Did you refuse to hand your phone to the teacher or place it in the caddy when asked? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, why did you refuse to do this? Explain your reason in full:

How many times were you asked before you completed this form? ______

Then the "what happened" tickbox section follows as Section C, the rule-explainer becomes D, and so on down. That way the refusal is confronted in writing first — and a written "why" on a dated form is far harder to walk back than a verbal "nah" in the moment. It also captures the refusal even in the exact scenario where they never gave the phone up, because the form documents the standoff itself.

Want me to show the full redraft with everything renumbered, then build it?

You said: they should also have the option to store in bag or locker however, if it is in either one of those places, students cannot take toilet breaks during class.

they should also have the option to store in bag or locker however, if it is in either one of those places, students cannot take toilet breaks during class. (am i being too strict?)

09:55

Claude responded: On the strictness question — no, I don't think that's unreasonable, but it's worth being clear-eyed about what the rule actually is, because the logic matters …

On the strictness question — no, I don't think that's unreasonable, but it's worth being clear-eyed about what the rule actually is, because the logic matters for defensibility.

What you're really doing is closing a loophole: the caddy is the verifiable option (you can see the phone is parked), while bag/locker is unverifiable (the phone could come to the toilet for a quiet scroll or a photo of a test). Tying "phone out of sight in your bag" to "then you don't leave the room with it" is a coherent, proportionate response to that — it's not punitive, it's removing the opportunity. Plenty of teachers run exactly this.

Two caveats worth holding, since you asked:

The medical/welfare exception has to survive. Some students have legitimate reasons to need the toilet promptly — continence conditions, menstruation, anxiety, diabetes, IBS. A blanket "no toilet if phone's in your bag" can't override those, and you don't want a parent or wellbeing coordinator able to say you denied a kid the toilet over a phone-storage technicality. The rule should bite on the discretionary break, not the genuine need — and the genuine-need kids shouldn't have to disclose why at the door.

The framing should be choice, not punishment. "Caddy = normal freedoms; bag/locker = you've chosen the option I can't verify, so the toilet privilege is paused" reads as a logical consequence the student controls. "No toilet because your phone's in your bag" reads as you weaponising the toilet, which is the version that generates complaints. Same rule, different defensibility.

So: not too strict, provided the toilet restriction is positioned as the trade-off for choosing the unverifiable storage option, and provided it yields to genuine welfare need without interrogation.

Here's how I'd write it into Section B:

Section B — Did you refuse to hand over your phone?

Did you refuse to put your phone in the caddy when asked? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, why did you refuse? Explain your reason in full:

Storing your phone: the caddy is the expected option. You may instead store your phone switched off in your bag or locker. However, if your phone is in your bag or locker rather than the caddy, you may not leave the room for toilet breaks during the lesson, as the phone is not in sight. (Genuine medical or welfare needs are an exception — speak to the teacher.)

Which did you choose today? ☐ Caddy ☐ Bag/locker (no toilet breaks this lesson) ☐ Refused all options

How many times were you asked before you completed this form? ______

The "Refused all options" tickbox is the important one — it cleanly separates a kid who's negotiating storage (fine) from a kid who's refusing the rule entirely (the real problem), and it routes them differently down the form.

Want me to show the full renumbered redraft now and build it?

You said: students with welfare or medical needs actually get a specific special permission so I will ask them whether they have that before handing out the form.

students with welfare or medical needs actually get a specific special permission so I will ask them whether they have that before handing out the form.

09:56

Claude responded: That's cleaner — the exception lives in your pre-screening, not the form.

That's cleaner — the exception lives in your pre-screening, not the form. So a student holding the form is, by definition, someone you've already confirmed doesn't have a medical/welfare pass. That lets the form state the toilet rule flatly, with no softening parenthetical, because the genuine-need cases never reach this paper.

It also tightens the document: no "speak to the teacher about exceptions" line for a savvy kid to lean on mid-lesson, and no implied invitation to disclose medical info on a form that goes home via Compass (which you wouldn't want anyway). The form stays purely about the choice and the breach.

Revised Section B with the exception removed:

Section B — Did you refuse to hand over your phone?

Did you refuse to put your phone in the caddy when asked? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, why did you refuse? Explain your reason in full:

Storing your phone: the caddy is the expected option. You may instead store your phone switched off in your bag or locker. However, if your phone is in your bag or locker rather than the caddy, you may not leave the room for toilet breaks during this lesson, because the phone is not in sight.

Which did you choose today? ☐ Caddy ☐ Bag/locker (no toilet breaks this lesson) ☐ Refused all options

How many times were you asked before you completed this form? ______

Want the full renumbered redraft now, then I'll build it into the matching Word doc?

You said: show me the whole thing again

show me the whole thing again

09:56

Claude responded: Mobile Phone & Device Incident Form

Mobile Phone & Device Incident Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you continue with the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Time of incident: ______

Section B — Did you refuse to hand over your phone?

Did you refuse to put your phone in the caddy when asked? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, why did you refuse? Explain your reason in full:

Storing your phone: the caddy is the expected option. You may instead store your phone switched off in your bag or locker. However, if your phone is in your bag or locker rather than the caddy, you may not leave the room for toilet breaks during this lesson, because the phone is not in sight.

Which did you choose today? ☐ Caddy ☐ Bag/locker (no toilet breaks this lesson) ☐ Refused all options

How many times were you asked before you completed this form? ______

Section C — What happened? (teacher will tick before handing you this form)

☐ Refused to place phone/device in the phone caddy when asked
☐ Used a phone/device in class without permission
☐ Took, or attempted to take, a photo/video/recording (e.g. of the whiteboard or notes)
☐ Made or received a phone call
☐ Used a phone/device to look up an answer instead of thinking through the task
☐ Wore or used a smart watch / earbuds / other device
☐ Other: _______________

Was a teacher's instruction to put the device away given before this form? ☐ Yes ☐ No

Section D — Know the rule

Read this carefully. You are expected to understand it after today.

In Victorian government schools, mobile phones and personal devices must be switched off and stored securely for the whole school day — not just "not used." This rule has applied across the state since 2020. From the start of 2027 it is law in every Victorian school, and it also covers smart watches and earbuds.

This means:

  • Refusing to put your phone away when asked is itself a breach, even if you weren't using it.
  • A phone only comes out for a task if the teacher has given the class permission for that specific activity. You do not decide this — the teacher does.
  • Using a phone to photograph notes, take a call, or look up an answer is not allowed.

After reading the above:
Did you already know this was the rule before today? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If you answered No, what did you previously believe the rule was?

Section E — Your account

In your own words, describe what you did with the device:

Why did you have the device out / why did you refuse to hand it over?

Did the teacher give the class permission to use phones for this task? ☐ Yes ☐ No

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If you have now read Section D and understand the rule, and this was a genuine first-time misunderstanding, tell the teacher — you are finished here. Hand the form in.

If you already knew the rule and chose to breach it, or you refused a direct instruction to hand the device over, you must complete Section F below.

Section F — Accountability (only if you knew the rule and chose to breach it, or refused an instruction)

If you used the phone to photograph notes or look up an answer: what skill or thinking were you avoiding by doing that, instead of doing it yourself?

How does a phone going off, a call, or a device being used affect the learning of other students in the room?

When a teacher gives a direct instruction and a student refuses, what does that do to the lesson?

What will you do with your phone at the start of the next lesson, and every lesson after?

Section G — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this incident will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass. I understand that repeated or serious breaches may be escalated.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
First-time misunderstanding ☐ Knew rule, chose to breach ☐ Refused direct instruction ☐
Device confiscated until end of lesson ☐ Returned to student ☐ Held at office ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

There's one small redundancy now worth flagging: Section B asks "why did you refuse" and Section E asks "why did you refuse to hand it over" again. That was fine when B was just a tickbox, but now that B captures the full written reason, the second ask in E is repetitive. Want me to cut that line from E (leaving E focused on phone use rather than refusal), or keep both as a deliberate consistency check? Once you've called that, I'll build it.

You said: get rid of how many times were you asked etc and re-present complete form for perusal

get rid of how many times were you asked etc and re-present complete form for perusal

Claude responded: Mobile Phone & Device Incident Form

Mobile Phone & Device Incident Form

This form must be completed in full, in pen, before you continue with the lesson. Do not interrupt the teacher.

Section A — Identification

Full name (first and surname): _______________
Year level: ______ Class/Subject: ______ Teacher: ______
Date: ______ Time of incident: ______

Section B — Did you refuse to hand over your phone?

Did you refuse to put your phone in the caddy when asked? ☐ Yes ☐ No

If YES, why did you refuse? Explain your reason in full:

Storing your phone: the caddy is the expected option. You may instead store your phone switched off in your bag or locker. However, if your phone is in your bag or locker rather than the caddy, you may not leave the room for toilet breaks during this lesson, because the phone is not in sight.

Which did you choose today? ☐ Caddy ☐ Bag/locker (no toilet breaks this lesson) ☐ Refused all options

Section C — What happened? (teacher will tick before handing you this form)

☐ Refused to place phone/device in the phone caddy when asked
☐ Used a phone/device in class without permission
☐ Took, or attempted to take, a photo/video/recording (e.g. of the whiteboard or notes)
☐ Made or received a phone call
☐ Used a phone/device to look up an answer instead of thinking through the task
☐ Wore or used a smart watch / earbuds / other device
☐ Other: _______________

Was a teacher's instruction to put the device away given before this form? ☐ Yes ☐ No

Section D — Know the rule

Read this carefully. You are expected to understand it after today.

In Victorian government schools, mobile phones and personal devices must be switched off and stored securely for the whole school day — not just "not used." This rule has applied across the state since 2020. From the start of 2027 it is law in every Victorian school, and it also covers smart watches and earbuds.

This means:

  • Refusing to put your phone away when asked is itself a breach, even if you weren't using it.
  • A phone only comes out for a task if the teacher has given the class permission for that specific activity. You do not decide this — the teacher does.
  • Using a phone to photograph notes, take a call, or look up an answer is not allowed.

After reading the above:
Did you already know this was the rule before today? ☐ Yes ☐ No
If you answered No, what did you previously believe the rule was?

Section E — Your account

In your own words, describe what you did with the device:

Did the teacher give the class permission to use phones for this task? ☐ Yes ☐ No

STOP. Read this before continuing.

If you have now read Section D and understand the rule, and this was a genuine first-time misunderstanding, tell the teacher — you are finished here. Hand the form in.

If you already knew the rule and chose to breach it, or you refused a direct instruction to hand the device over, you must complete Section F below.

Section F — Accountability (only if you knew the rule and chose to breach it, or refused an instruction)

If you used the phone to photograph notes or look up an answer: what skill or thinking were you avoiding by doing that, instead of doing it yourself?

How does a phone going off, a call, or a device being used affect the learning of other students in the room?

When a teacher gives a direct instruction and a student refuses, what does that do to the lesson?

What will you do with your phone at the start of the next lesson, and every lesson after?

Section G — Declaration

I confirm the information above is true and complete. I understand this incident will be recorded and that a notification may be sent to my parent/guardian via Compass. I understand that repeated or serious breaches may be escalated.

Student signature: _______________ Date: ______

For teacher use only:
First-time misunderstanding ☐ Knew rule, chose to breach ☐ Refused direct instruction ☐
Device confiscated until end of lesson ☐ Returned to student ☐ Held at office ☐
Compass entry logged ☐ Parent notified ☐ Escalate to YLC/coordinator ☐
Notes: _______________

I also cut the duplicate "why did you refuse to hand it over" line from Section E, since Section B now captures that reasoning in full — E is focused purely on phone use. Want me to build this into the matching Word doc now?

Claude responded: Page 1 is clean and matches the late form's house style.

Ran 2 commands, created a file, viewed a file

Page 1 is clean and matches the late form's house style. Let me confirm the STOP gate and back sections.

Checking page 2

Both pages render cleanly and the whole thing fits on two pages with the teacher box closing it out. The STOP gate sits right where it should, separating the universal account from the gated accountability section.

Presented file

Here's the phone form as an editable Word doc, in the same house style as the late form — two pages, with the STOP gate dividing the universal account (A–E) from the gated accountability section (F).

Two things to flag for when you put it into use:

The Section D rule statement is accurate as of now: phones must be stored, the policy has held since 2020, and the 2027 date is when it becomes legislated for all schools including non-government ones. If you're handing these out into 2027, that "from the start of 2027 it is law" line will read as present-tense rather than upcoming — worth a quick edit then.

The "what skill were you avoiding" question in Section F is still the sharp one I flagged earlier. It's aimed squarely at your outsourcing-of-thinking concern, but it's the most likely to draw a blank or a smart-arse answer from a disengaged kid. Easy to swap later if it doesn't land.

You now have a matching pair. Want the Compass post templates to go with them — short, factual wording for the home notification in each case?

Phone incident form
Document · DOCX 

You said: okay next up.

okay next up. I want to make a google form. Can you handle that?

10:11

Claude responded: I can't create a live Google Form directly — there's no Google Forms tool connected here, so I can't push a form into your Google account and hand you a link.

I can't create a live Google Form directly — there's no Google Forms tool connected here, so I can't push a form into your Google account and hand you a link.

What I can do, depending on what you're after:

The fastest route to an actual working form is Google Apps Script — I write you a script, you paste it into script.google.com, run it once, and it builds the entire form in your Drive automatically (all sections, questions, multiple-choice options, the conditional "go to section" logic). This is the closest thing to me building it for you. Takes about two minutes on your end and you get a real, editable Google Form.

Alternatively I can give you a clean build sheet — every question, type, and option laid out in order so you can paste them into the Forms UI yourself. Slower, but no script involved.

One thing worth thinking through before either: Google Forms handles the conditional "STOP — only continue if..." gate differently from paper. On paper the student self-selects. In a Form, you'd use section branching — e.g. the answer to "Did you already know the rule?" routes them either to submit or onward to the accountability section. That's actually more robust than paper since they can't skip ahead. But it means the form's logic needs designing around branch points rather than a visual STOP bar.

Which form is this — the late one, the phone one, or a new one? And is this for students to fill out on a device, or for you to log incidents yourself?

You said: that's great, let's make the script.

that's great, let's make the script.

I want to ask about the Power Booklet. Do you remember we made that?

10:14

Claude responded: Two separate things here, so let me take them in turn.

Two separate things here, so let me take them in turn.

On the script — happy to build it. I just need to know which form it's for (late arrival, phone incident, or something new) and whether it's the student filling it in on a device or you logging incidents yourself. That choice changes the branching logic, so tell me which and I'll write the Apps Script.

On the Power booklet — let me check rather than rely on a vague impression.

Relevant chats

Yes, I've got it. The Power booklet is the stimulus collection of original articles you built — 13 of them, with your own titles: Superhero Fatigue, The Real Deal (Bluey), Language Death, Passport Power, Veto Power, The Chip That Runs the World, The Attention Economy, Brain Drain, The Euphemism, Rare Earth Minerals, The Supporting Act, The Unpaid Internship, and The Tip. Each article has accompanying vocab and cloze work, and you'd developed the "power chain" concept — geographic → knowledge → resource → manufacturing → technological → economic → soft → psychological power — plus a node-and-edge connection map where students argue why two articles link.

It was anchoring an EAL ELT (open-booklet assessment, 20 min planning / 60 min writing) alongside the mainstream "The Power of Choice" task.

What did you want to do with it — extend it, revise something, build the connection map, work on the assessment, or something else?

And so I can knock out the Apps Script in the same go: which form, and student-filled or you-logging?

You said: i want to get student reflections on it via a form.

i want to get student reflections on it via a form.

10:14

Claude responded: Good — a student reflection form on the Power unit.

Good — a student reflection form on the Power unit. That's a clean fit for Google Forms, and there's no branching-logic complexity to wrestle with, so the script will be straightforward.

Before I write it, the design question is what you actually want the reflection to do, because that changes the questions. A few angles it could take:

Reflection on the content/ideas — which articles landed, what shifted their thinking about power, which connections they found surprising. This mines the conceptual payoff of the unit.

Reflection on their learning/skills — how they found the writing task, what they did well, where they struggled, what they'd do differently. More metacognitive, useful for reports and feedback.

Reflection on the unit design itself — feedback to you on what worked pedagogically, pacing, difficulty, which articles to keep or cut next time. This is you gathering intel to improve the unit.

Most reflection forms blend the first two and quietly fold in a bit of the third. But tell me where you want the weight, and whether this is for your EAL cohort, mainstream, or both — because the language level of the questions should match. EAL students need shorter, more scaffolded prompts; mainstream can handle more open, abstract ones.

What's the priority?

You said: the priority is mainly around assessing the tool.

the priority is mainly around assessing the tool. The tool was designed to provide context on power in our world and to support with the generation of ideas.

10:15

Claude responded: That sharpens it nicely.

That sharpens it nicely. You're not after a generic "what did you learn" reflection — you're evaluating whether the booklet did its two jobs: building background knowledge about power, and helping students generate ideas for their own writing. That's a tool-evaluation survey with the student as the data source, not a reflection-for-its-own-sake exercise.

So the questions should map to those two functions and test them honestly:

Did it build context/knowledge? Did students arrive at the task knowing more about how power works in the world? Which articles actually delivered that, and which fell flat? Did anyone come in already knowing this stuff (ceiling effect) or find it over their heads (floor effect)?

Did it help generate ideas? This is the one that matters most for you, since idea-generation was the design intent. Did they actually draw on it while planning and writing, or did it sit unused? Could they find an angle, or did they stare at it? Did the connection-map / power-chain thinking help them link ideas, or was that lost on them?

Plus the honest-feedback layer — which articles to keep, what was too hard, whether the open-booklet condition helped or just gave them too much to wade through under time pressure.

One design call before I build: do you want this anonymous or named? Anonymous gets you franker feedback on the tool (kids will admit "I didn't use it" more readily), but named lets you cross-reference a student's reflection against the actual piece they produced — which is far more powerful for working out whether self-reported usefulness matches real performance. For tool-assessment specifically, I'd lean named with a line reassuring them it's about the booklet, not their marks. Your call.

And is this EAL, mainstream, or both? If both, I'd build one form with the question wording pitched so EAL students can access it, rather than two forms — unless you want them separated for cleaner data.

You said: Q: Named or anonymous responses?

Q: Named or anonymous responses? A: Anonymous (franker tool feedback)

Q: Which cohort is this for? A: Both (one accessible form)

10:30

Claude responded: Anonymous, one accessible form for both cohorts.

Anonymous, one accessible form for both cohorts. That settles the design: question wording pitched so EAL students can access it without dumbing it down for mainstream, no name field, and a mix of scaled questions (quick, give you quantifiable data) and short open ones (give you the why).

Here's the question set I'll build into the script. Scan it before I write the code — easier to adjust now than after.

Intro text: This survey is about the Power booklet — the articles you used during the unit. It is not about your marks or your writing. I want to know honestly whether the booklet helped you, so I can make it better. Your answers are anonymous.

Part 1 — Building knowledge about power

  1. Before this unit, how much had you thought about how power works in the world? (scale: Not at all → A lot)
  2. After using the booklet, how much better do you understand how power works in the world? (scale: Not at all better → Much better)
  3. Which articles helped you the most? (checkboxes — all 13 titles)
  4. Were any articles too hard to understand? (checkboxes — all 13 titles + "None")
  5. What is one thing about power you understand now that you didn't before? (short answer)

Part 2 — Generating ideas for your writing
6. When you were planning your writing, how much did you use the booklet? (scale: Didn't use it → Used it a lot)
7. The booklet helped me find ideas to write about. (agree scale)
8. The booklet helped me connect different ideas together. (agree scale)
9. Without the booklet, finding ideas to write about would have been… (scale: Much harder → Much easier)
10. Did you use the connections between articles — the way one topic links to another — when forming your ideas? (Yes / A little / No / I didn't notice there were connections)

Part 3 — Making it better
11. Having the booklet open during the task was: (Very helpful / Helpful / A bit distracting — too much to read / I didn't use it)
12. Which articles should I keep for next year? (checkboxes — all 13)
13. What would make the booklet more useful for you? (short answer)
14. Anything else you want to tell me about the booklet? (short answer, optional)

A couple of notes on deliberate choices:

Questions 1 and 2 are a matched before/after pair — that lets you measure self-reported knowledge gain, which is the cleanest evidence the "context" function worked. Same trick with 6–9 for the "idea generation" function.

Question 10 is the honest test of your connection-map / power-chain concept. It includes "I didn't notice there were connections" as an option, because that's a real and useful answer — if a chunk of the class ticks it, the map needs more scaffolding, and you'd want to know that rather than have it hidden.

Question 4 (too hard) matters for your EAL cohort especially — it surfaces floor effects without singling those students out, since it's anonymous and everyone answers it.

Want me to adjust anything — add, cut, or reword — or build the script as is?

You said: not all of the articles we made wound up in the booklet: The definitive list is: Where do Ideas Come From?

Claude responded: Twelve articles, final list:

Twelve articles, final list:

  1. Where Do Ideas Come From?
  2. Passport Power
  3. Veto Power
  4. The Chip That Runs the World
