I'll build the Apps Script now with this list in the three checkbox questions (most helpful / too hard / keep for next year).

Created a file, read a file

Here's the script. The setup steps are written as comments at the top of the file, but quickly:

Go to script.google.com, make a new project, paste the whole file in, hit Save, then Run. The first run throws a permissions dialog — that's just Google checking whether the script may create a form in your Drive; click through Advanced → Go to project → Allow. Run it once more, then open the execution log and you'll find two links: the live form to send students, and an edit link to tweak it yourself.

A few things worth knowing about the choices baked in:

It's set to allow more than one response per device (setLimitOneResponsePerUser(false)), because anonymous forms with email collection off can't reliably enforce one-per-person anyway, and if students share a class set of devices you don't want the second kid locked out. The trade-off is a determined student could submit twice — fine for low-stakes reflection, but flag if you'd rather lock it down.

The matched before/after scale pairs (Q1–Q2 for knowledge, Q6–Q9 for idea generation) are what give you measurable evidence each function worked, rather than just vibes. When the responses come in, Forms will show you the distributions automatically, and you can pull everything into Sheets with one click for the cross-tabs.

The "I did not notice there were connections" option on the connections question is the honest diagnostic for your power-chain/map concept — if that one gets a lot of ticks, the connections need surfacing more explicitly next year.

Once you've run it and seen the form, you might want to reorder or reword directly in the Forms editor — that's easier than re-running the script. But if you'd rather I change questions, just tell me and I'll hand you a revised version.

Want the Compass post templates for the late and phone forms now, or is that for another day?

Power reflection form
GS 

You said: what do i do at this point to activate it?

what do i do at this point to activate it?

10:45

Claude responded: Two issues to spot here, and they explain why nothing seems to have happened.

Two issues to spot here, and they explain why nothing seems to have happened.

First, the function selector at the top says myFunction, not createPowerReflectionForm. The log shows "Execution started / completed" with no link lines because it ran an empty myFunction, not the form builder. So the form wasn't actually created.

Here's the fix:

Click the function dropdown (where it says myFunction, next to the Debug button) and select createPowerReflectionForm from the list. Then click Run again.

If createPowerReflectionForm doesn't appear in that dropdown, it means the code didn't fully paste or save — Ctrl+S to save first, then check the dropdown.

This time the first run will likely trigger the permissions dialog (Review permissions → pick your account → Advanced → Go to project → Allow). Run once more after granting, and the execution log will then print the two link lines you can see in the code at 145–146 — the live form URL and the edit URL.

To confirm: after a successful run, the log will show two extra lines starting with "LIVE FORM" and "EDIT FORM," each with a link. The live one is what you send students. You'll also find the form sitting in your Google Drive.

Ignore the Deploy button entirely — that's for web apps and add-ons, not for this. You never need it here.

You said: this: createPowerReflectionForm, is not findable in that list

this: createPowerReflectionForm, is not findable in that list

10:46

Claude responded: That means the code in the editor isn't the form script — it's still showing the default myFunction, or the paste didn't land properly.

That means the code in the editor isn't the form script — it's still showing the default myFunction, or the paste didn't land properly. The execution log running an empty function confirms it.

Quick diagnosis: look at the very top of Code.gs, around line 1. If you scroll up, does the file actually contain function createPowerReflectionForm() { somewhere? The dropdown only lists functions it can find in the saved file, so if the name isn't in there, the function isn't there.

Most likely what happened: when you pasted, the original function myFunction() {} stub was left in and the paste went somewhere that broke it, or only part pasted. Cleanest fix is to start fresh:

Select everything in the Code.gs editor (click in the editor, Ctrl+A) and delete it so the file is completely empty. Then paste the full script again from the top. Make sure the very first line is the /** comment block and that you've got the whole thing through to the final closing } on line 148.

Then press Ctrl+S to save. Saving is what refreshes the function dropdown — until you save, it won't see the new function name.

After saving, click the dropdown and createPowerReflectionForm should now be there. Select it, hit Run, and clear the permissions dialog when it appears.

If it's still not showing after a clean paste and save, tell me — paste me the first few lines that are actually in your editor and I'll see what's going on. One common culprit: if only the comment block pasted and not the function body, the dropdown stays empty because comments contain no runnable functions.

You said: so can i use Google's Apps Script to build and make apps?

so can i use Google's Apps Script to build and make apps?

14:45

Claude responded: Yes — Apps Script is a real development platform, not just a form-builder.

Yes — Apps Script is a real development platform, not just a form-builder. What you just used is the thin end of it. Here's the honest scope of what it can and can't do.

What it's genuinely good for

It's built to automate and extend Google Workspace, so its sweet spot is anything touching Docs, Sheets, Forms, Gmail, Drive, and Calendar. You can write scripts that generate documents, process spreadsheet data, send templated emails, build forms (as you've seen), and wire these together into workflows. For a teacher, that's a lot of real mileage: auto-generating mail-merge reports from a marks spreadsheet, emailing parents off a Sheet, building a roll-marking system, turning form responses into formatted documents.

It can also build actual web apps — you write HTML/CSS/JavaScript for the front end and Apps Script for the back end, deploy it, and get a URL people can visit. So things like a class tool, a data-entry interface, a small dashboard, a quiz app — all doable and hostable for free on Google's infrastructure. That Table Jumbler seating randomiser you've built before is exactly the kind of thing that could live as an Apps Script web app instead.

And it builds add-ons — custom menus and sidebars inside Docs/Sheets/Forms, which is how you'd make a tool your whole faculty could install.

The honest limits

It's not a general-purpose app platform. It's slow for heavy computation, has execution-time quotas (scripts get killed after ~6 minutes), and daily limits on things like emails sent. It's JavaScript only. It's not where you'd build a polished standalone product, a mobile app, or anything needing a real database at scale — it leans on Sheets as a quasi-database, which is fine for hundreds of rows, not millions. And the web apps it produces look functional rather than beautiful unless you put real front-end work in.

So the rule of thumb: if the task orbits Google Workspace, Apps Script is often the fastest route that exists. If it doesn't — if you're building something standalone that has nothing to do with your Drive — you'd usually want a different tool.

Given what you build — classroom tools, the seating randomiser, marking and curriculum workflows — a fair amount of it sits squarely in Apps Script's wheelhouse. I can write you these the same way I just did the form: I hand you the script, you paste and run.

What did you have in mind? If you've got a specific tool in your head, tell me and I'll tell you straight whether Apps Script is the right home for it or whether something else fits better.

You said: okay so let's say I want to create a sociology interactive activity which asks students to drag and drop movable rectangles with descriptors inside them into e…

okay so let's say I want to create a sociology interactive activity which asks students to drag and drop movable rectangles with descriptors inside them into either the left square labelled 'deviant but not illegal' or the right square 'illegal but not deviant' and then I wanted to copy a link onto the classroom for students to access and play that, could I?

14:47

Claude responded: Yes, that's completely doable — and it's a good example of where you have two routes, one of which is much better than the other for your situation.

Yes, that's completely doable — and it's a good example of where you have two routes, one of which is much better than the other for your situation.

The Apps Script route would work but is the wrong tool here. Apps Script web apps are clunky for smooth drag-and-drop, they load slowly behind a Google login wall, and the interaction feels stiff. Drag-and-drop is exactly the kind of rich front-end interaction Apps Script is weakest at. So I'd steer you off it for this one.

The better route: a single HTML file. A drag-and-drop sorting activity like this is pure front-end — HTML, CSS, JavaScript, no server, no database, no logins. That means it can be one self-contained file that runs in any browser. This is genuinely the ideal use case for it.

The question that decides everything is how you host it so students get a clickable link, since a file on your computer won't have a URL. A few honest options:

GitHub Pages (free, gives you a real link): you put the HTML file in a free GitHub repo, switch on Pages, and you get a permanent public URL like yourname.github.io/deviance-sort that you paste into Compass/Google Classroom. Best long-term answer — set up once, reuse forever, edit anytime. Slight learning curve the first time.

Netlify Drop (free, fastest): you drag the HTML file onto netlify.com/drop and it instantly gives you a live URL. Zero setup, no account strictly needed. The catch is the URL is randomish and the free-tier permanence is less guaranteed than GitHub.

Google Sites (you already have it): you can embed an HTML file into a Google Site and share that link. Stays in your Google ecosystem, but embedding is slightly fiddlier and the activity sits inside Google's page chrome.

Here's the part that matters for a sorting activity: do you want it to just let them sort freely, or should it check their answers and give feedback (mark which they got right/wrong, give a score)? That's the difference between a manipulative and a self-marking quiz, and it changes how I build it. For sociology specifically, the interesting pedagogical move is that some items are genuinely contestable — "deviant but not illegal" vs "illegal but not deviant" has fuzzy cases — so you might not want hard right/wrong marking, and instead want a "reveal and discuss" button that shows your intended placement plus a note on why it's debatable.

Two quick things and I'll build it:

You said: Q: How should the activity handle answers?

Q: How should the activity handle answers? A: Free sort, then 'reveal & discuss' (shows intended answers + notes)

Q: Which hosting route suits you? A: GitHub Pages (permanent, reusable)

14:48

Claude responded: Good combination — and the "reveal & discuss" model is the right pedagogical call for deviance/illegality, because the fuzzy cases are where the actual sociolo…

Good combination — and the "reveal & discuss" model is the right pedagogical call for deviance/illegality, because the fuzzy cases are where the actual sociology lives. Let me build the activity first, then walk you through GitHub Pages.

I'll need content. I can build it with a solid default set of items that I'll choose to illustrate the concept well, and you can edit the text directly in the file afterward (I'll make that part easy to find and change). The classic teaching examples for this two-box distinction:

Deviant but not illegal: picking your nose in public, jumping a queue, standing too close to someone in a lift, belching loudly at dinner, talking loudly on speakerphone on a train, an adult only ever eating with their hands.

Illegal but not deviant: jaywalking, exceeding the speed limit by a few km/h, downloading pirated music, underage sips of alcohol at a family dinner, using a phone while stopped at lights, mild littering like an apple core.

The pedagogically rich part is that "not deviant" is culturally relative — jaywalking is unremarkable in Melbourne but socially deviant in some countries — which is exactly what your reveal notes can draw out. Let me build it with these, plus discussion notes on each, and you swap any you don't like.

Reading frontend-design skill before building the interactive

I'll build this with a distinctive design rather than a generic sorting widget — something that suits a sociology classroom and reads as intentional. Let me create it.

Building the deviance/illegality drag-and-drop sorting activity as a self-contained HTML file

I left a couple of stray characters in a CSS variable. Let me fix that.

Fixing corrupted CSS variable line

Let me take a screenshot to confirm it renders correctly.


Claude's response was interrupted.

You said: Illegal but not generally considered deviant

Illegal but not generally considered deviant These are acts that break the law but are often viewed as normal, understandable, or relatively acceptable by large numbers of people:

  • Driving 5–10 km/h over the speed limit.
  • Jaywalking where it is technically prohibited.
  • Downloading copyrighted movies, music, or TV shows.
  • Sharing streaming service passwords (where prohibited by terms or law).
  • Rolling through a stop sign rather than making a complete stop.
  • Crossing train tracks in unauthorized locations.
  • Parking slightly beyond a time limit.
  • Taking office supplies home from work.
  • Using a mobile phone briefly while driving in jurisdictions where this is prohibited.
  • Underage drinking in cultures where it is socially tolerated.
  • Drinking alcohol in public places where it is prohibited.
  • Keeping found money rather than attempting to locate the owner.
  • Failing to declare small amounts of income for tax purposes.
  • Using software without a valid license.
  • Littering small items such as cigarette butts.
  • Riding a bicycle on a footpath where prohibited.
  • Fishing without the correct permit.
  • Bringing food across borders without declaring it.
  • Trespassing in abandoned buildings out of curiosity.

  • Burning copyrighted media for friends. Most people may acknowledge these are technically illegal while not viewing the offender as a "deviant" person. Deviant but not illegal These violate social expectations but are not crimes:

  • Wearing a tuxedo to a supermarket.
  • Talking loudly to yourself in public.
  • Refusing to shake hands when it is culturally expected.
  • Standing facing the wrong way in an elevator.
  • Eating breakfast foods at midnight (mildly deviant in some contexts).
  • Dressing far outside local fashion norms.
  • Having an extremely unconventional hairstyle.
  • Naming a child something highly unusual (within legal limits).
  • Remaining silent throughout a social gathering.
  • Never celebrating birthdays or holidays.
  • Refusing to use social media.
  • Living in a vehicle despite having other options.
  • Talking to strangers extensively on public transport.
  • Reading children's books as an adult.
  • Collecting unusual objects such as bottle caps or road signs.
  • Eating insects in cultures where that is uncommon.
  • Walking barefoot through a city.
  • Choosing not to marry or have children when that is strongly expected.
  • Practising unusual religious or spiritual beliefs that are legal.
  • Quitting a prestigious career to pursue a niche hobby.
  • Maintaining a nocturnal sleep schedule.
  • Living as a recluse.
  • Speaking in an archaic or highly formal manner in everyday conversation.
  • Refusing all modern technology.
14:50

Claude responded: That's a much richer item bank than the dozen I built with — and several of yours are pedagogically sharper because they sit right on the fault line (sharing s…

That's a much richer item bank than the dozen I built with — and several of yours are pedagogically sharper because they sit right on the fault line (sharing streaming passwords, keeping found money, refusing to shake hands, quitting a prestigious career). I'll expand the activity to draw from your full set.

One design caution before I just dump all 44 in: a drag-and-drop with 44 tiles is unwieldy on screen — students spend more time scrolling than thinking, and the two columns become a wall. Better options:

The cleanest approach is to put your full list in the file as the pool, but have the activity randomly draw a subset (say 10–12, balanced across both categories) each time it loads. That way the tile count stays manageable, every play is different, and you've still got the whole bank embedded. Replay value built in — good for running it more than once or across multiple classes.

Alternatively I just load all 44 and accept the long scroll. Workable but clunkier.

I'd go with the random-subset approach. Let me also flag two of your items worth a second look, since you'll be defending placements to a sharp Year 11:

"Drinking alcohol in public places where it is prohibited" — you've filed it under illegal-but-not-deviant, which is defensible, but in many Australian contexts public drinking is also seen as somewhat deviant, so it's a borderline case. Fine to keep, but it's one where the reveal note should acknowledge the ambiguity rather than assert it cleanly.

"Trespassing in abandoned buildings out of curiosity" — similar; urbex is arguably deviant and illegal for many people, so it muddies the clean two-box logic. These contestable ones are great discussion fuel but you might want them flagged as "deliberately arguable" in the notes.

Let me rebuild with your full bank, the random-subset draw, and tightened reveal notes. Quick check:

You said: Q: How many tiles per play?

Q: How many tiles per play? A: Draw a random 12 each time (balanced, replayable)

Q: Reveal notes — how much do you want written for each item? A: Just the borderline ones need notes; simple ones can have short notes

14:50

Claude responded: Good — random 12 each play (balanced 6/6), concise notes on simple items and fuller notes on the genuinely contestable ones.

Good — random 12 each play (balanced 6/6), concise notes on simple items and fuller notes on the genuinely contestable ones. Let me rebuild with your full bank.

Edited 2 files

Now I need to update the build() function to do the balanced random draw.

Updating build to draw a balanced random subset

Now I need to add the currentCount variable and fix the score to use it instead of ITEMS.length.

Edited 2 files

Let me verify it renders correctly by opening it in a headless browser and screenshotting.

Ran 2 commands

12 tiles, as configured. Let me look at it.

Viewing the rendered activity

That's rendering cleanly — the colour-coded headline (deviant in red, illegal in teal), the two clearly-labelled zones, draggable tiles with the hand-drawn shadow style, and a balanced 6/6 draw from your full bank. The activity works end to end. Let me hand you the file.

Presented file

There's your activity. Open it in any browser to test it — drag tiles into the two boxes, hit Reveal & discuss, and each tile gets a green outline (matched your intended answer) or red (didn't), with the discussion note appearing underneath. It draws a fresh balanced 12 each time it loads, so it replays differently. Works on touch screens too, for tablets.

A couple of things you can edit directly in the file (open it in any plain text editor — Notepad is fine):

The DRAW = 12 line near the start of the script controls how many tiles appear. Change it to draw more or fewer, or set it to 0 to load all 44 at once. The full item bank sits just below that — add, cut, or reword any item, just keep the answer:'left' (deviant) / answer:'right' (illegal) tags right.

The "score" is deliberately worded as "X / 12 match the intended answer" rather than "correct," because — as the borderline items prove — there isn't always one right answer. That phrasing keeps the door open for a student to argue your placement was the debatable one, which is the discussion you want.

Now, GitHub Pages, to turn this into a link for Compass. First time takes about ten minutes; every activity after is faster. Here's the walkthrough:

First, make a free account at github.com if you don't have one. Then:

  1. Click the + top-right → New repository. Name it something like sociology-activities. Set it to Public (Pages needs public on free tier). Tick Add a README. Click Create repository.
  2. In the new repo, click Add file → Upload files. Drag in deviance_sort.html. Scroll down, click Commit changes.
  3. Go to the repo's Settings tab → Pages in the left sidebar. Under "Branch," select main, leave folder as /(root), click Save.
  4. Wait 1–2 minutes. Refresh the Pages settings page and a link appears at the top: https://yourusername.github.io/sociology-activities/. Your activity is at that URL plus the filename: https://yourusername.github.io/sociology-activities/deviance_sort.html.

That last URL is what you paste into Compass or Google Classroom. It's permanent, free, and editable — to change the activity, upload a new version of the file to the repo and the link updates automatically.

One catch worth knowing: GitHub Pages URLs are public to anyone with the link. There's nothing sensitive in a sorting activity so that's fine here, but don't put anything confidential (student data, marks) in a Pages repo.

Want me to build the Compass post templates next, or is there another activity you want to make while the hosting's fresh in your head?

Deviance sort
Code · HTML

You're in the right spot — you've got the + menu open. Click New repository (second item in that dropdown).

That takes you to the repo creation page. There you'll:

  • Repository name: type something like sociology-activities
  • Public/Private: make sure Public is selected (Pages needs this on the free tier)
  • Tick Add a README file
  • Click the green Create repository button at the bottom

Then come back and I'll walk you through uploading the HTML file. Go ahead.

Claude responded: The repo MSCsociology is created — good.

The repo MSCsociology is created — good. It looks like you didn't tick "Add a README," so it's empty, but that's fine, we can upload straight in. Ignore all the command-line stuff in those grey boxes — you don't need any of it.

In your second screenshot, find the line that says "Get started by creating a new file or uploading an existing file."

Click uploading an existing file (the blue link).

That takes you to an upload page. Drag your deviance_sort.html file onto it (or click "choose your files" and select it). Then scroll down and click the green Commit changes button.

15:03

Claude responded: One snag: the file uploaded as sociology game.

One snag: the file uploaded as sociology game.txt, not as an .html file. Two problems with that — it has a .txt extension (so browsers will show the raw code, not run the activity) and a space in the name (which makes for an ugly URL). The browser needs it to end in .html to render it as a web page.

Easiest fix is to rename it. Here's how:

  1. Click on sociology game.txt in the file list to open it.
  2. On the file view, click the pencil icon (Edit) near the top right.
  3. At the top you'll see the filename in a box. Change it from sociology game.txt to deviance.html (no spaces, ends in .html).
  4. Click Commit changes (green button, top right), then Commit changes again in the popup.

That renames it. Then tell me and we'll switch on Pages.

One thing worth checking first, though — when you uploaded, did the file content actually go in as the full HTML? If you opened the original file and copied text into a .txt, it may have got mangled. The cleanest path, if you're unsure: delete sociology game.txt, and re-upload the original deviance_sort.html file I gave you directly (drag the actual file in without opening or renaming it first). But try the rename first — if it works, we're fine.

15:04

Claude responded: Good — you've now got sociology game.

Good — you've now got sociology game.html uploaded, which is the one we want. You've got a leftover sociology game.txt sitting there too; it's harmless but let's tidy it up so it doesn't confuse you later.

Two small jobs, then we turn on Pages.

First, the spaces in the filename. sociology game.html has a space, which makes a messy URL (sociology%20game.html). Worth renaming to remove it:

  1. Click sociology game.html to open it.
  2. Click the pencil (Edit) icon.
  3. Change the filename box to deviance.html.
  4. Commit changes (green button), confirm.

Second, delete the stray .txt (optional but tidy):

  1. Click sociology game.txt.
  2. Click the trash/bin icon near the top right.
  3. Commit changes.

Then turn on Pages:

  1. Click Settings (top right of the repo menu).
  2. In the left sidebar, click Pages.
  3. Under "Branch," select main from the dropdown, leave the folder as /(root), click Save.
  4. Wait 1–2 minutes, refresh that page, and a link appears at the top.

Your activity will live at that link plus /deviance.html on the end. Paste that into Compass.

Do the rename and the Pages step, then paste me the link it gives you and I'll confirm the exact full URL to use.

You're in great shape — better than you think. Look at the screen: the file is now named sociologygame.html (no spaces, ends in .html), and the code is all there starting with <!DOCTYPE html>. That's exactly what we want. The rename worked.

So the file is done. Now you just need to switch on Pages. Here's exactly where to click:

  1. Near the top of the page, in the row that starts with Code · Issues · Pull requests…, click Settings (it's the last item, with the gear icon, on the right).
  2. That loads a new page with a long sidebar on the left. Scroll down that left sidebar until you see Pages. Click it.
  3. You'll see a section called "Build and deployment." Under Branch, there's a dropdown that probably says "None." Click it and choose main.
  4. Leave the folder dropdown next to it as /(root).
  5. Click Save.

Then wait 1–2 minutes, refresh that same Pages page, and a box will appear at the top saying something like "Your site is live at https://benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology/".

Perfect — you're on exactly the right page. You can see "GitHub Pages is currently disabled." Let's enable it.

  1. Click the None dropdown (the one just left of the Save button, under "Branch").
  2. From the list that drops down, select main.
  3. A second dropdown will appear next to it for the folder — leave it as /(root).
  4. Click Save.

Ignore the "Visibility / GitHub Enterprise / Start free for 30 days" section entirely — that's a paid upsell you don't need.

After you click Save, the page will reload. Wait about a minute, then refresh it — a green box will appear near the top saying "Your site is live at..." with your link.

That's the base URL — it'll show a 404 or a blank/README page on its own, because there's no file called index.html in the repo. Your activity lives at a specific file, so you need the filename on the end:

That full address — with sociologygame.html at the end — is the one that loads the drag-and-drop activity, and the one you paste into Compass.

One optional tidy-up for later, if you want the bare URL to work too: you could rename sociologygame.html to index.html, and then the base link (the one you just pasted) would load the activity directly, no filename needed. But that's cosmetic — the full link works fine as is, so don't bother unless you care about a cleaner URL.

You said: great, can we do the exact format and style for a new 'website' / 'game' this time on folkways and mores?

great, can we do the exact format and style for a new 'website' / 'game' this time on folkways and mores?

15:19

Claude responded: Good — same drag-and-drop format, same visual style, this time sorting behaviours into folkways vs mores.

Good — same drag-and-drop format, same visual style, this time sorting behaviours into folkways vs mores. That's a natural companion piece to the deviance/illegality one, and the same "reveal & discuss" model fits beautifully because the folkway/more line is exactly where the interesting sociology sits.

Quick refresher on the distinction so we're building to the right concept, since it's a touch more subtle than deviant/illegal:

Folkways are everyday norms of custom and etiquette — manners, conventions, "the done thing." Breaking one makes you rude, odd, or embarrassing, but not immoral. Facing the wrong way in a lift, wearing pyjamas to the shops, eating spaghetti with your hands.

Mores (more-rayz) are norms with serious moral weight — violating them makes you bad, not just awkward. They carry strong emotional condemnation and often underpin laws. Cheating on a partner, cutting in front of someone at a funeral, lying under oath, neglecting a child.

The pedagogically rich part — the reason "reveal & discuss" works — is that the line is genuinely contestable and shifts over time and culture. Tattoos were once a more violation, now barely a folkway. Spitting in public sits ambiguously between the two. That ambiguity is the discussion you want students having.

Before I build, two things:

The deviance activity used a red/teal palette. For this one I'd shift the colour scheme so students don't confuse the two activities at a glance — say a warm amber for folkways (light, everyday, low-stakes) and a deep purple for mores (weighty, moral). Same layout and mechanics, different identity. Sound good, or do you want it visually identical?

And the items. I can write a full balanced bank myself — I've got plenty of strong examples across the spectrum including the deliberately-borderline ones. Or, like last time, you might have your own list you'd rather use. Do you want me to draft the items, or will you supply them?

You said: Folkways (minor norms)

Folkways (minor norms)

  • Saying "please" and "thank you"
  • Queuing and waiting your turn
  • Greeting people when entering a room
  • Wearing appropriate clothing for the occasion
  • Covering your mouth when you cough
  • Using cutlery in the expected way
  • Not talking loudly during a movie
  • Making eye contact during conversation
  • Bringing a small gift when invited to dinner
  • Holding a door open for someone behind you
  • Standing a socially acceptable distance from others
  • Raising your hand before speaking in class
  • Returning a shopping trolley
  • Sending a thank-you message after receiving a gift
  • Not interrupting conversations
  • Taking your hat off in situations where that is customary
  • Waiting for everyone to be served before eating
  • Using indoor rather than outdoor voices
  • Wearing shoes in public places

  • Not staring at strangers Breaking these norms might make people think, "That's strange," but rarely, "That's morally wrong." Mores (major norms)

  • Not stealing
  • Not physically assaulting others
  • Not cheating on a spouse or partner
  • Not lying under oath
  • Respecting the dead
  • Caring for dependent children
  • Not abusing vulnerable people
  • Respecting personal consent
  • Not driving while intoxicated
  • Not engaging in serious fraud
  • Not abandoning family responsibilities
  • Not exploiting people financially
  • Not deliberately spreading dangerous misinformation
  • Not desecrating places of worship or memorials
  • Not discriminating against people in ways society strongly condemns
  • Helping someone in immediate danger when reasonably able
  • Not betraying deeply held trust
  • Respecting bodily autonomy
  • Not engaging in incest (in most societies)
  • Not committing acts viewed as fundamentally cruel

Visually distinct from the deviance activity so students won't mix them up, but unmistakably the same family. Here's the file.

The underscore is fine and makes a clean URL — just upload it exactly as named and that link will work. You said: so how do i embed this code and therefore this activity in my github?

15:31

Claude responded: You've actually already done it — that's what the upload step is.

You've actually already done it — that's what the upload step is. There might be a small confusion in the wording, so let me clarify what "embedding" means here, because there are two different things people mean by it.

If you mean: get the activity hosted on GitHub so it has a link — that's just uploading the file, which you already did for the deviance activity. For this new one: go to your MSCsociology repo, click Add file → Upload files, drag in folkways_mores.html, click Commit changes. Done. Because Pages is already on, it's live in a minute at the link I gave you. The HTML file is the activity — all the code (the layout, the drag-and-drop, the items) lives inside that single file, so uploading the file uploads everything. There's nothing separate to "embed."

If you mean: put the running activity inside another web page — for example, embedding it into a Google Site, a WordPress/blog page, or a Compass page so it appears within that page rather than as a link students click away to — then you use an <iframe>. That's a one-line snippet that drops the live activity into another page:

html
<iframe src="https://benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology/folkways_mores.html" width="100%" height="900" style="border:none;"></iframe>

You'd paste that into wherever the host platform accepts embed/HTML code. Google Sites has an "Embed" option that takes either a URL or this snippet; WordPress has a "Custom HTML" block; some Compass page editors allow it, some don't.

So which are you trying to do — just host it for a link (upload, already sorted), or display it inside another page (iframe)? If it's the iframe route, tell me the platform you're embedding into and I'll give you the exact steps for that one.

Claude responded: You've got it exactly right — that's precisely the process, and yes, you do need to upload the new file.

You've got it exactly right — that's precisely the process, and yes, you do need to upload the new file. This Settings/Pages screen isn't where you do it, though. You're on the hosting-settings page; uploading happens over in the Code tab. You don't need to touch anything on this Pages screen — it's already done its job (you can see "Your site is live at..." which confirms hosting is on).

Here's where to go:

Step 1 — get the file. The folkways/mores HTML file I made is already saved and ready — it's the folkways_mores.html I shared with you a moment ago. You don't need to create it yourself or copy any code; just download that file to your computer if you haven't already. (It should be in your Downloads.)

Step 2 — go to the Code tab. At the top of this page, click Code (the <> icon, far left of the menu row). That takes you to your repo's file list, where you currently see README.md, sociologygame.html, etc.

Step 3 — upload. Click the Add file button (top right of the file list) → Upload files. Drag folkways_mores.html in from your Downloads. Scroll down, click the green Commit changes.

That's it. Because Pages is already live (this very screen confirms it), the new file goes online automatically within a minute. No need to come back to Settings, no need to re-save anything.

Your new activity will then be at:

https://benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology/folkways_mores.html

So: click Code at the top, then Add file → Upload files. Go ahead, and tell me once it's committed.

15:35

Claude responded: I think the confusion is a wording loop — let me clear it up plainly, because you're not missing a step.

I think the confusion is a wording loop — let me clear it up plainly, because you're not missing a step.

GitHub is the website you're looking at right now. github.com — this whole thing, your MSCsociology repo, this Settings page — that's GitHub. There's no separate GitHub place to go to. You're already in it.

So when I say "upload the file," I mean upload it here, into the GitHub repo you're already looking at. Same site, just a different tab within it. Here's the entire thing in one move:

  1. On this exact page, click Code at the top (the <> icon, far left of the row that has Code · Issues · Pull requests · Actions…).
  2. That shows your list of files. Click Add file (top-right button) → Upload files.
  3. Drag folkways_mores.html from your computer's Downloads folder into the box.
  4. Click the green Commit changes button.

That's the whole job. The file is now in GitHub, and because hosting is already switched on, it's live at the link within a minute.

The only thing you need from outside GitHub is the file itself — folkways_mores.html — which I made for you and which should be sitting in your computer's Downloads. You don't write it, copy code, or create it. You just drag that one file into the Code tab.

Does that resolve it? If you're unsure whether the file is actually in your Downloads, tell me and we'll sort that first before you upload.

You said: so github is a claude thing?

so github is a claude thing?

15:35

Claude responded: No — GitHub has nothing to do with Claude or Anthropic.

No — GitHub has nothing to do with Claude or Anthropic. They're completely separate companies.

GitHub is a website owned by Microsoft. It's where millions of programmers store and share code, and it's been around since 2008, long before Claude existed. I just recommended it to you earlier as a free way to host your HTML activities so they'd have a clickable link for Compass — but it's an independent service you signed up for yourself with your own account (benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys). I can't see it, log into it, or put files into it for you. That's why you're the one clicking through it.

So the division of labour is:

Me (Claude): I write the HTML file for your activity and hand it to you as a download.

You + GitHub: You take that file and upload it into your GitHub repo, which hosts it online and gives you the public link.

They don't connect to each other directly — you're the bridge. I make the file, you carry it over to GitHub.

Think of it like this: I'm the person who writes you a document, and GitHub is the noticeboard you pin it to so others can read it. Two different services; you move the paper from one to the other.

Does that clear it up? If so, the next move is just getting the folkways_mores.html file I made into your GitHub repo — and the first question is simply whether that file is sitting in your computer's Downloads folder yet. Want to check that?

You said: but we uploaded the last one through Github together?

but we uploaded the last one through Github together?

Claude responded: I get why it feels that way, but no — you did all the GitHub clicking yourself.

I get why it feels that way, but no — you did all the GitHub clicking yourself. I was watching through your screenshots and telling you where to click, but my hands were never on it. You were the one in your GitHub account pressing the buttons.

Here's what actually happened with the deviance activity, step by step:

  1. I made the HTML file and handed it to you as a download.
  2. You downloaded it to your computer.
  3. You went to your GitHub repo and clicked Add file → Upload files.
  4. You dragged the file in and clicked Commit changes.
  5. You turned on Pages in Settings.

My whole role was reading your screenshots and saying "click that button next." Guidance, not access. I can't actually log into your GitHub or move files there — I have no way to reach it.

So "we did it together" is true in the sense that I coached you through every click. But every actual action inside GitHub was yours. That's exactly how this second one works too: I've already made the folkways_mores.html file (it's done, shared with you), and now you repeat the same clicking you did last time — Code tab → Add file → Upload files → drag it in → Commit.

The one thing to confirm before you start: is the folkways_mores.html file in your computer's Downloads folder? When I shared it a few messages back, you'd click it to save it down. If it's there, you're ready to upload it exactly like last time. If you're not sure it saved, tell me and we'll get that sorted first.

That's completely fine — the name doesn't matter, only that it ends in .html, which it does. folkways.html works perfectly.

15:37

Claude responded: Right — a 404 means the file isn't actually in the repo yet.

Right — a 404 means the file isn't actually in the repo yet. Most likely you tried the link before uploading the file, or the upload didn't complete. The link can only work once folkways.html is sitting in your repo's file list.

Let's verify and fix. First, let's check whether it uploaded at all:

Go to https://github.com/benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys/MSCsociology (your repo's Code tab). Look at the list of files. Do you see folkways.html in that list, alongside sociologygame.html and README.md?

If it's NOT there — the upload didn't happen. Do it now:

  1. Click Add file (top right) → Upload files.
  2. Drag folkways.html from your Downloads into the box.
  3. Scroll down, click the green Commit changes button. (This last click is the step people most often miss — dragging the file in isn't enough; you must commit.)
  4. Wait about a minute, then reload the folkways.html link.

If it IS there — then check the spelling/case matches exactly. The URL says folkways.html all lowercase. If the file in the repo is Folkways.html or has a space or different spelling, that's the mismatch. Click the file, use the pencil to rename it to exactly folkways.html, commit.

Have a look at the repo file list and tell me whether folkways.html is there. That tells us which path we're on.

You said: we need to update the mores because they all begin with 'not' which makes the game too easy.

we need to update the mores because they all begin with 'not' which makes the game too easy.

15:41

Claude responded: Sharp catch — that's a real design flaw.

Sharp catch — that's a real design flaw. Nearly every more is phrased as "Not stealing," "Not lying," "Not assaulting," while every folkway is phrased as a positive action ("Saying please," "Holding a door open"). A student doesn't even need to understand the concept; they can just sort by "does it start with Not?" and ace it. The grammar leaks the answer.

The fix is to rephrase the mores as the behaviour itself or a neutral noun phrase, so both columns read the same grammatically. So instead of "Not stealing from others," the tile says "Stealing from someone" — and the student has to actually judge whether stealing is a folkway-level or more-level norm violation. That also makes the activity sit more naturally with the deviance one, where tiles describe acts.

There's one thing to get right when you flip them, though: the tile has to describe the act that breaks the norm, framed neutrally, so the two boxes still make sense. The zone labels already handle the framing — "Folkway: breaking it seems rude" vs "More: breaking it is seen as genuinely wrong" — so a tile reading "Stealing from someone" correctly belongs in More, because stealing breaks a moral norm. That's consistent. Let me show you the rephrased mores:

  • Stealing from someone
  • Physically assaulting someone
  • Cheating on a partner
  • Lying under oath
  • Desecrating the dead
  • Neglecting a dependent child
  • Abusing a vulnerable person
  • Ignoring someone's consent
  • Driving while drunk
  • Committing serious fraud
  • Abandoning your family responsibilities
  • Financially exploiting someone
  • Deliberately spreading dangerous misinformation
  • Desecrating a place of worship or memorial
  • Discriminating in ways society strongly condemns
  • Ignoring someone in immediate danger you could help
  • Betraying deeply held trust
  • Violating someone's bodily autonomy
  • Incest
  • Doing something seen as fundamentally cruel

Each now describes the norm-breaking act, matching the folkways grammatically — and the notes underneath still explain why it's a more, so the reveal still teaches.

One consequence worth a thought: a couple of these now overlap conceptually with the deviance/illegality activity (stealing, assault, drink-driving also appear in legal/illegal territory). That's fine — it's actually good reinforcement that the same act can be analysed through different sociological lenses — but if you'd rather keep the two activities cleanly distinct, I can swap those few for mores that aren't also crimes (cheating on a partner, betraying trust, incest, cruelty, abandoning family — the "wrong but not necessarily illegal" ones, which are the most pedagogically interesting mores anyway).

Want me to rebuild the file with these rephrasings as-is, or lean it toward the not-also-illegal mores to keep it distinct from the deviance game?

You said: Folkways

Folkways

  • Greeting people when you arrive.
  • Waiting your turn in a queue.
  • Wearing appropriate clothing for a job interview.
  • Bringing a small gift to a dinner party.
  • Speaking quietly in a library.
  • Returning a shopping trolley after use.
  • Covering your mouth when coughing.
  • Sending a thank-you note after receiving a gift.
  • Standing an appropriate distance from others during conversation.
  • Raising your hand before speaking in class.
  • Arriving on time for a social event.
  • Holding a door open for someone behind you.
  • Taking shoes off before entering a home where that is customary.
  • Waiting until everyone is served before beginning a meal.
  • Using table manners while eating.
  • Offering your seat to an elderly passenger on public transport.
  • Wearing black or other subdued colours to a funeral.
  • Keeping your phone on silent during a performance.
  • Cleaning up after yourself in a shared kitchen.

  • Introducing guests to one another at a gathering. Mores

  • Caring for dependent children.
  • Respecting the property of others.
  • Remaining faithful to a marriage partner.
  • Providing honest testimony in court.
  • Respecting the physical safety of others.
  • Obtaining consent before intimate activity.
  • Protecting vulnerable people from harm.
  • Honouring serious promises and commitments.
  • Treating people fairly regardless of background.
  • Respecting the dignity of the deceased.
  • Supporting family members in times of genuine need.
  • Acting honestly in financial dealings.
  • Respecting the privacy of personal information.
  • Taking responsibility for harm you have caused.
  • Showing compassion toward people experiencing hardship.
  • Respecting the bodily autonomy of others.
  • Exercising authority responsibly when entrusted with power.
  • Protecting community members from foreseeable danger.
  • Acting with integrity when entrusted with public office.
  • Providing care for those who cannot care for themselves.
15:42

Claude responded: This is a much better revision — and you've solved the grammar leak in a more elegant way than I'd suggested.

This is a much better revision — and you've solved the grammar leak in a more elegant way than I'd suggested. Instead of describing the norm-breaking act, you've framed both columns as the upheld norm — positive statements of what's expected ("Caring for dependent children," "Respecting the property of others"). Now both columns read identically in grammar and tone, and the student genuinely has to weigh moral seriousness rather than spot a linguistic tell.

There's one consequence to flag before I build, because it affects the zone labels. The tiles now describe the norm being kept, not broken. So the current zone subtitles — "breaking it seems rude" / "breaking it is seen as genuinely wrong" — still work, but the framing question shifts subtly: the student is sorting "is this expectation a folkway or a more?" rather than "is this violation minor or serious?" That's actually a cleaner conceptual task, and it matches how the terms are properly taught. I'll just tweak the zone subtitles so they fit the upheld-norm framing rather than the violation framing:

  • Folkway — an everyday custom; ignoring it seems rude or odd, not immoral
  • More — a norm with deep moral weight; violating it is seen as genuinely wrong

That keeps the logic airtight. Let me rebuild the file with your 20 folkways, 20 mores, and updated notes.

Updating zone subtitles to fit upheld-norm framing

Now let me also update the intro lede to match the upheld-norm framing, then replace the item bank.

Edited 2 files, ran a command, viewed a file

That's the fix working exactly as intended. Both columns now read in identical grammar — "Respecting the privacy of personal information," "Wearing appropriate clothing to a job interview," "Remaining faithful to a marriage partner" — so there's no grammatical tell. A student can't shortcut it by spotting "Not"; they have to actually judge moral weight. The intro and zone labels match the upheld-norm framing. Here's the updated file.

Presented file

There's the revised activity. Test it locally, and you'll see both columns are now grammatically indistinguishable — the game actually requires understanding the concept.

Now for getting it onto GitHub, since the earlier 404 told us the file wasn't in the repo yet. This is the same upload you did for the deviance activity, and it replaces whatever attempt happened before:

  1. Download this new folkways_mores.html to your computer (it'll go to Downloads). Note the name — if your browser saved the old one as folkways.html, this one may save as folkways_mores.html or folkways_mores(1).html. Whatever it's actually called, that's what determines your link.
  2. Go to your repo: github.com/benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys/MSCsociology
  3. Click Add file → Upload files.
  4. Drag the file in.
  5. Click the green Commit changes button — this is the step that actually saves it. Dragging alone doesn't do it.

Then your link is the base URL plus the exact filename. If it uploads as folkways_mores.html, the link is:

https://benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology/folkways_mores.html

To avoid the earlier confusion: after committing, look at your repo's file list and check the exact name shown. Tell me what it says and I'll give you the precise link to test. The 404 last time almost certainly meant the commit step didn't complete — so watch for that green Commit changes button and make sure you click it.

Folkways mores
Code · HTML 

You said: alright the next one I want to talk it through with you.

alright the next one I want to talk it through with you. Firstly, it will be ethos, pathos, logos, but the english teacher has requested that she can monitor who has grasped it, i.e. the results, so is there a way we can get name input and a teacher view page of the results

15:47

Claude responded: Good — this is a real step up from the sorting activities, and it's worth thinking through properly before building, because "the teacher can see results" is t…

Good — this is a real step up from the sorting activities, and it's worth thinking through properly before building, because "the teacher can see results" is the requirement that changes the whole architecture.

The core problem: the sorting games are pure front-end — everything happens in the student's browser and vanishes when they close the tab. That's fine when there's no data to keep. But "monitor who has grasped it" means results have to leave the student's browser, get stored somewhere, and be readable by the teacher later. A single HTML file on GitHub Pages can't do that on its own — GitHub Pages is read-only hosting, it can't receive and save data. So we need somewhere for the data to land.

You've got a few genuinely different routes, and they trade off in ways worth understanding:

Route 1 — Google Forms as the engine (least build, most robust). Don't build a custom app at all. Make the ethos/pathos/logos activity as a Google Form — name field, then questions where students classify examples. Responses flow automatically into a Google Sheet the teacher owns, with names, scores, timestamps, and per-question breakdowns. Forms even auto-marks if you make it a quiz. The English teacher likely already knows Forms, can open the results herself, and there's no hosting or code to maintain. The cost: it looks and feels like a form, not a slick interactive — no drag-and-drop, less visually engaging.

Route 2 — Custom interactive that submits to a Google Sheet (best of both, more setup). Build the engaging custom activity like the sorting games, but wire it to send each student's results to a Google Sheet via a small Apps Script "web app" acting as the receiving endpoint. Students get the nice interface; the teacher gets a live Sheet of results. The cost: more moving parts to set up once (a script, a deployment, a URL pasted into the HTML), and if it breaks later it's harder for a non-coder to fix.

Route 3 — Custom interactive with a results database (most powerful, most overkill). A service like Firebase or Supabase stores results and powers a real teacher dashboard page. This is what you'd build if this were going school-wide as a product. For one teacher monitoring one class, it's far more than you need.

There's also a privacy angle worth naming now, since it's student data: with names attached, results shouldn't sit on a public URL anyone could find. Routes 1 and 2 keep the data inside the teacher's Google account (private) even if the activity itself is public — that's the right shape. A public dashboard page showing named scores would not be.

My honest steer: Route 1 if the teacher mainly wants the data and is comfortable in Google's tools — it's dramatically less to build and maintain, and "monitor who grasped it" is exactly what Forms quiz mode does well. Route 2 if the engaging interactive experience matters as much as the data — worth the extra setup, but you're taking on a bit of maintenance.

Before I go further, three things shape which way to build:

You said: Q: What matters more for this one?

Q: What matters more for this one? A: Both equally

Q: How tech-comfortable is the English teacher with things like Google Sheets? A: Yes, she's comfortable in Google tools

Q: What should the activity actually ask students to do? A: Classify given examples into E/P/L

15:58

Claude responded: Both matter equally, the teacher's comfortable in Google tools, and the task is classification — that combination points cleanly at Route 2: the engaging custo…

Both matter equally, the teacher's comfortable in Google tools, and the task is classification — that combination points cleanly at Route 2: the engaging custom interactive plus results flowing to a Google Sheet she owns. Her Google-comfort is what makes Route 2 viable without you becoming permanent tech support, because when she wants the data she just opens her own Sheet.

Let me lay out exactly how this will work before I build anything, because the architecture has a few parts and you should understand the shape — especially since you'll be the one setting up the connecting piece.

The three components:

The activity itself is a custom interactive, same family as your sorting games but built for three buckets instead of two: ethos, pathos, logos. Students classify example statements ("Buy this watch — 9 out of 10 cardiologists recommend it" → logos/ethos territory, deliberately arguable). It opens with a name field they must fill before starting. Lives on GitHub Pages like the others.

The receiver is a Google Apps Script web app — a small script that sits behind a URL and does one job: when the activity finishes, it catches the student's name, score, and answers, and writes a row into a Google Sheet. You set this up once in the teacher's (or your) Google account, paste its URL into the activity, and it's done.

The results are just a normal Google Sheet — one row per student, with name, score, timestamp, and how they answered each item. The teacher opens it like any spreadsheet. Live, sortable, no dashboard to build.

What this means for the rhetoric content itself: classification of ethos/pathos/logos has a wrinkle the deviance game didn't — many real persuasive examples use two or three appeals at once. "As a doctor and a mother, I'm terrified of what this does to children" is ethos (doctor) + pathos (terrified, children). So I'd build it so each example has one primary intended answer for scoring, but the reveal note flags where an example legitimately blends appeals. That keeps the marking clean for the teacher's data while preserving the honest "it's often more than one" lesson. Worth deciding: do you want pure single-answer items (cleaner data, slightly less true to rhetoric) or a primary-answer-with-blend-notes approach (truer, but a student could reasonably dispute a mark)?

The privacy shape: the activity is public (anyone with the link can open it), but the results live inside the Google account that owns the script and Sheet — not on any public page. That's the correct arrangement for named student data. One caveat to be aware of and I'll build around: because the activity is public, the receiver URL is technically visible to a curious student viewing the page source, meaning a determined kid could submit fake entries. For a formative "has she grasped it" check that's a low stakes risk, but you should know it's there — it's the trade-off for not putting it behind a login.

Two decisions and one logistics question before I build:

You said: Q: How should items handle examples that use more than one appeal?

Q: How should items handle examples that use more than one appeal? A: Primary answer + blend notes (truer to rhetoric)

Q: Whose Google account should own the script and results Sheet? A: My account, I'll share the Sheet with her

Q: Should students see the correct answers after they finish? A: Reveal answers + notes after submitting (learning tool)

16:00

Claude responded: Good decisions all round — primary-answer-with-blend-notes is the intellectually honest choice, your account owning the data keeps it clean (you share the Shee…

Good decisions all round — primary-answer-with-blend-notes is the intellectually honest choice, your account owning the data keeps it clean (you share the Sheet with her, revoke if she leaves, etc.), and reveal-after-submit means it teaches as well as measures. The reveal happens after the result is already recorded, so a student seeing the answers can't retroactively improve their logged score — the data captures their genuine first attempt.

I'll build this in two parts and walk you through assembly. Let me do the activity first, then the receiver script. Let me start the build.

Copying base activity for the rhetoric build

Three buckets instead of two changes the layout and the engine more than the previous swaps did, so rather than patch the two-zone file piecemeal, let me build this one properly from the structure up — a name gate, three drop zones, submission to the Sheet, then reveal. I'll write it fresh.

Building the ethos/pathos/logos classification activity with name gate and Google Sheet submission

I copied the file earlier. Let me overwrite it.

Ran 2 commands, created a file, viewed a file

That's working — three colour-coded zones (green ethos, rose pathos, blue logos), all 15 examples shown, name gate confirmed working since the activity only appeared after I entered a test name. Note it loads all 15 here rather than drawing a subset, because for an assessment you want every student answering the same items so the teacher's data is comparable. Let me verify the gate screen too.

Viewing the name gate

Both states work. The name gate blocks the activity until a name is entered, then the three-zone classifier appears. The activity is built and ready — but right now it runs in "practice mode" because the SHEET_URL is still empty (it'll show students their score and notes but won't record anything). That's intentional: the activity works on its own, and we plug in the results-recording separately.

Here's the file for the activity itself:

Presented file

That's the activity. You can open it right now and test it — enter a name, sort the examples, hit Submit & reveal, and you'll see the score and the blend notes. It just won't save anywhere yet.

Now the second half: the receiver that captures results into a Google Sheet. Here's how the whole thing fits together, then I'll give you the script.

The flow once it's all connected: student enters name → sorts → submits → the activity quietly sends {name, score, answers, timestamp} to a Google Apps Script URL → the script writes a row into a Google Sheet you own → you (and the teacher you share it with) see results build up live.

Here's the setup, which you do once:

Step 1 — make the Sheet. Go to sheets.google.com, create a blank spreadsheet, name it something like "EPL Results." Leave it empty; the script creates the header row.

Step 2 — open its Apps Script. In that Sheet, click Extensions → Apps Script. This opens a script editor bound to that specific sheet (different from the standalone one you used for the form — this one is attached to the sheet).

Step 3 — paste the receiver script (below), Save.

Step 4 — deploy it as a web app. Click Deploy → New deployment → gear icon → Web app. Set "Execute as: Me" and "Who has access: Anyone." Click Deploy, authorise when asked. It gives you a web app URL.

Step 5 — paste that URL into the activity's SHEET_URL = "" line, re-upload the HTML to GitHub. Done — results now flow.

Let me write you the receiver script.

Created a file, read a file

Both files are ready. The receiver script writes each submission as a row: timestamp, name, score, total, percent, and the full breakdown of how they classified each example — so the teacher can see not just who struggled but which appeal they confused (e.g. a student who keeps filing statistics under ethos has a specific, nameable misunderstanding). That per-question detail is more diagnostic than a bare score.

The order to do things, so you're never debugging two unknowns at once:

First, test the activity alone — open the HTML now, run through it. It works in practice mode (shows score and notes, saves nothing). Confirm you're happy with the examples and the feel before wiring anything up.

Then, when ready, set up the receiver following the steps in the .gs file's comments — make the Sheet, paste the script via Extensions → Apps Script, deploy as a web app, copy the URL.

Then connect them — paste that URL into the SHEET_URL = "" line in the HTML (between the quotes), save, and upload to GitHub. From that point results flow to your Sheet, and you share the Sheet with the English teacher (Share button, her email, "Viewer" or "Commenter").

A couple of honest caveats worth knowing before you commit to this for real assessment:

The "Who has access: Anyone" setting on the web app is what lets students' browsers reach it without logging in — but it also means the URL accepts data from anyone who has it. As I flagged, a tech-curious student could view the page source, find the URL, and submit junk. For a formative "has she grasped it" check this is an acceptable risk; for anything that counts toward a grade, you'd want the more locked-down database route instead. Match the tool to the stakes.

And because the name is free-typed, you'll get the usual mess — "Jack," "jack s," "Jacko" — so the teacher may need to tidy names when reading results. A dropdown of class names would fix that but makes the file class-specific; worth it only if she's running it repeatedly with fixed classes.

Want me to walk you through the receiver setup now, step by step like we did with GitHub, or would you rather test the activity first and come back to the wiring when you've got the Sheet open?

Ethos pathos logos
Code · HTML 
Epl results receiver
GS
