15:49

Claude responded: Yes — but GitHub doesn't put an obvious "view website" button on this page, which is the frustrating part.

Yes — but GitHub doesn't put an obvious "view website" button on this page, which is the frustrating part. There's no link here that takes you straight there. A couple of routes:

The reliable way — just type the address. Click into your browser's address bar (where it currently says github.com/...) and type:

benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology

Hit enter. That's your live site. It's worth bookmarking it now so you never have to hunt for it again.

The route that's actually on this page (buried): scroll down on the right-hand side, under "About / Releases," there's a section that will say Deployments (you can just see it starting at the very bottom of your screenshot — "Deployment" is appearing). Click into that and it links to the live github-pages URL. But honestly, typing the address is faster.

Everything's uploaded correctly, by the way — I can see index.html, diamond_ranking.html, and all the others sitting in the repo, all committed. So the site is live and current. Type that address and you'll land on the Thinkery with all three sociology cards including the new diamond. Bookmark it when you get there.

You said: it's just that that website doesn't exactly roll off the tongue or stick in my mind

it's just that that website doesn't exactly roll off the tongue or stick in my mind

15:50

Claude responded: Fair — benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.

Fair — benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/MSCsociology is a mouthful, and it's not something you'd want to read aloud to a class or print on a worksheet. You've got a few options, honestly ranging from free-and-quick to paid-and-permanent.

Free, five minutes — rename the repo to something shorter. GitHub lets you rename the repo (Settings → scroll to "Repository name"). If you renamed MSCsociology to, say, thinkery, your URL becomes benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys.github.io/thinkery — shorter, and it matches the brand. The benjaminedmondrobertcoady-sys part is stuck, though, because that's your GitHub username. Which leads to the bigger lever:

Free — change your GitHub username. That long username is the real problem. If you made a second GitHub account (or renamed this one) to something like thinkeryapp or mscthinkery, and named the repo thinkery, you'd get thinkeryapp.github.io/thinkery. Cleaner, but renaming a username can break existing links and is a bit of a faff.

The proper fix — a custom domain (small annual cost). This is what makes it truly memorable. You buy a domain like thethinkery.au or thinkery.education (roughly $15–25/year from a registrar), and point it at your GitHub Pages site. Then the address is simply thethinkery.au — nothing else. Rolls off the tongue, fits on a worksheet, sticks in the mind, looks professional to other teachers. GitHub supports this for free; you only pay for the domain name itself. It's the standard way people give a Pages site a real address.

Free workarounds if you don't want to pay or fiddle: a URL shortener (bit.ly lets you make a custom link like bit.ly/msc-thinkery), or — simplest of all — just bookmark it and put it as a clickable link in Compass, so neither you nor the students ever type it. For classroom use, a clickable Compass link is often all you actually need.

My honest steer: if this is mainly for your own classes, bookmark it + a Compass link costs nothing and solves the day-to-day problem entirely. If you're imagining other teachers using it and want it to feel like a real thing, a custom domain (~$20/year) is the move that makes "the Thinkery" a name people can actually find and remember — and it's the natural next step now that the site has an identity worth giving a proper address.

Want me to walk you through renaming the repo to thinkery (the free quick win), or talk through how a custom domain would work if you're tempted by that?

You said: so i love the style.

so i love the style. I wonder if i could request a mid 90s windows education/ utopian scholastic style

10:07

Claude responded: Oh, that's a rich and specific aesthetic — and a real one, not just nostalgia.

Oh, that's a rich and specific aesthetic — and a real one, not just nostalgia. Let me make sure I'm seeing the same picture you are before I touch the design, because "mid-90s Windows educational" has several distinct flavours and they don't all mix.

What I think you're reaching for is the visual world of mid-90s edutainment and scholastic software — the era of Encarta, the Microsoft Home range, early educational CD-ROMs, school computer labs running Windows 3.1/95. The "utopian scholastic" part is the key qualifier: not the grey corporate Windows of spreadsheets, but the optimistic, knowledge-is-wonderful dialect of it — the sense that a CD-ROM could contain the whole world and learning was a bright, exploratory adventure.

The concrete visual ingredients of that look:

The chrome — beveled grey buttons with that raised 3D edge (light top-left, dark bottom-right), title bars, window borders, the whole "everything is a panel" feel. Times New Roman or MS Serif for headings, system fonts everywhere. The Encarta palette — deep blues and teals, that specific purple-blue gradient, marble textures, brushed-metal headers. Clip-art optimism — globes, columns, open books, laurel wreaths, lightbulbs, little gold stars. A sense of classical-meets-digital — Greek columns and busts rendered in 256 colours, "the academy reborn in cyberspace." Faux-3D, drop shadows, gradient fills, starfield backgrounds. The CD-ROM-era promise that knowledge was a treasure you explored.

This actually suits the Thinkery conceptually — the name already gestures at a classical academy, and this aesthetic is "the classical academy as imagined by 1996 software." There's a real harmony there.

Before I rebuild, a few forks that genuinely change the result:

You said: Q: Which flavour of the mid-90s look?

Q: Which flavour of the mid-90s look? A: Encarta / CD-ROM encyclopedia (blue-teal, marble, scholarly)

Q: How far do you want to push it? A: Full pastiche — lean in hard, beveled buttons and all

Q: Reskin scope? A: Splash + all activities (whole site reskin)

10:08

Claude responded: Excellent — full Encarta pastiche, leaning in hard, across the whole site.

Excellent — full Encarta pastiche, leaning in hard, across the whole site. That's a fun brief. Let me do the splash page first as the flagship — get the aesthetic exactly right there — and once you've approved the look, I'll roll the same treatment across the activities, since they share a visual language and I'd rather nail it once than reskin five files in a direction you don't love.

Let me reach for the right visual ingredients: the deep blue-to-teal gradients, the marble and brushed-metal header bars, beveled 3D buttons, gold-star scholarly accents, serif headings, and that "knowledge is a treasure to explore" optimism. Let me build it.

Backing up the current splash page before the reskin

I've backed up your current clean version (so we can always return to it). Now the Encarta build.

Ran 2 commands, created a file, viewed a file

That's the full Encarta pastiche, and it's leaning in exactly as you asked. The whole thing is a 1996 application window: title bar with minimise/maximise/close buttons, the File/Edit/View/Explore/Help menu strip, that deep navy-to-teal hero gradient with the radiating sunburst in the corner, the gold "Interactive Multimedia Activities" badge with its beveled edge, five gold stars, and the parchment content area. Subjects are "Chapters" with Roman numerals in gold tiles, each activity an encyclopedia entry with a beveled thumbnail icon and a chunky "OPEN ▸" button that inverts when pressed. Even a Windows status bar along the bottom with inset cells. The serif headings and MS-Sans-Serif body text complete it.

One small thing I notice: the headline reads "Welcome to the / The Thinkery" — a doubled "the." Easy fix. And I should flag a real concern before you commit to rolling this across everything:

The emoji icons (⚖️ 🤝 💎 🏛️) are a slight anachronism — they didn't exist in 1996, and they render in full modern colour, which breaks the period illusion a little. For true pastiche I could replace them with CSS-drawn faux-clip-art (a pixel globe, a column, etc.) in 256-colour style. Worth doing if you want it airtight, but it's more work — flag me if you care.

Let me fix the doubled "the":

Edited a file, read a file

There's the Encarta-style splash page. Before you upload it and before I reskin the five activities to match, have a proper look and tell me if this is the direction — because once I roll it across everything, that's five more files in this style, and I want to be sure you love it first.

A few things to weigh as you look:

The emoji icons are the one anachronism, as I mentioned — modern full-colour emoji in a 1996 frame. If you want it airtight I'll swap them for CSS faux-clip-art. If you don't mind the small wink, they stay and it's less work.

The "OPEN ▸" / double-click language sells the era but is slightly playful-fictional (it's a normal web link, not really a double-click). That's part of the charm of full pastiche, but worth knowing it's a deliberate bit of theatre.

And the honest design trade-off: this look is characterful but busier and lower-contrast in places than the clean version — the MS-Sans-Serif body text is small, and dense navy-on-parchment is less effortless to read than the airy modern layout was. For a splash page that's fine, it's a feast for the eyes. For the activities themselves, where students need to focus on sorting tiles and reading examples, the heavy chrome could compete with the task. So one option worth considering: Encarta splash page as the showpiece front door, but keep the activities clean (or lightly themed), so the chrome welcomes them in but doesn't clutter the actual thinking. The "lobby vs serious rooms" idea again.

So three questions before I go further: does the splash look land for you, do you want the emoji swapped for period clip-art, and do you want the activities fully chromed to match or kept cleaner for focus? Upload this one whenever you're happy with it (replaces index.html), and tell me how to play the activities.

Claude responded: Fair enough — and good to know now rather than after I'd reskinned five files.

Fair enough — and good to know now rather than after I'd reskinned five files. Let me figure out what's off rather than guess and rebuild blind.

What I gave you was the Windows-application reading of the brief — title bar, menu strip, status bar, the whole "it's a program running in a window" conceit. That might be the bit that's wrong: the chrome makes it look like 1996 software, when what you may actually be after is the 1996 scholastic / utopian feeling without it being dressed as an app window.

Let me get a clearer picture. When you imagine the look you want, which of these is closer:

The dreamy, idealised classical end — think the inside cover of a 90s encyclopedia, or the painted "knowledge is wonderful" imagery: soft gradients, marble, columns, golden light, a sunrise-over-learning optimism. More painterly than computery.

Or the CD-ROM menu screen end — the actual screen you'd see when an educational CD booted up: a rich illustrated background, glowing buttons, a "choose your adventure into knowledge" layout, but not wrapped in Windows chrome.

Or something else entirely — maybe it's a specific thing you have in your mind's eye (a particular program, a textbook, a vibe) that I'm not landing on.

It'd help most if you could tell me what specifically felt wrong about what I made — was it the Windows window-frame stuff, the colours, the fonts, the icons, the overall mood? Even "it felt too much like a spreadsheet program and not enough like a magical learning world" would point me the right way.

And if there's an image, screenshot, or even a named piece of software/media that captures what you mean, describe it or show me — that's the fastest route to getting it right.

You said: how about Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft?

how about Gemeinschaft and Gesellschaft? Is there an activity we could build around that? if so, what graphic organiser would it be?

12:48

Claude responded: Good pivot — and yes, this is a rich one to build around.

Good pivot — and yes, this is a rich one to build around. Let me think through the concept first, because the nature of the Gemeinschaft/Gesellschaft distinction points quite clearly to which organiser fits, and it's not the obvious one.

The concept, quickly, to anchor the design. Tönnies' distinction: Gemeinschaft ("community") is social bonds rooted in personal relationships, tradition, kinship, shared values — the village, the family, the congregation. Gesellschaft ("society/association") is bonds rooted in impersonal, instrumental, contractual relationships — the city, the corporation, the bureaucracy. The classic sociology move is that modernisation shifts societies from Gemeinschaft toward Gesellschaft.

Why the obvious organiser is the wrong one. Your instinct might be a two-column sort (Gemeinschaft vs Gesellschaft), like the deviance game. But that's pedagogically weak here, and for a specific reason: most real relationships and institutions are not purely one or the other — they're a blend, and crucially, the interesting sociology is how much of each, not which bin. A family business is Gemeinschaft bonds inside a Gesellschaft structure. A binary sort forces a false choice and hides the actual insight.

The organiser that fits: the continuum/spectrum. This is the one you flagged earlier as a favourite, and Gemeinschaft/Gesellschaft is almost the textbook case for it. You put a line:

Pure Gemeinschaft ←——————————————→ Pure Gesellschaft
(personal, traditional, communal) (impersonal, instrumental, contractual)

and students place relationships/institutions along it, not in boxes. A family dinner sits hard left; buying coffee from a stranger sits hard right; but a long-running local footy club, a small family firm, your relationship with a GP you've seen for twenty years — these sit in contested middle positions, and that's the discussion. Where exactly does a workplace friendship sit? Has your neighbourhood moved rightward over time?

This does three things a sort can't: it captures degree not just category, it surfaces that things are blends, and it lets you run the killer follow-up — "drag the marker to show where you think Australian society as a whole sits, and whether it's moving." That's Tönnies' actual thesis made physical.

An even richer variant, if you want it: a two-axis version isn't really warranted here (the concept is genuinely one-dimensional), so resist that. But you can layer a before/after move onto the continuum: place each item where it sat 50 years ago vs today, and the rightward drift becomes visible. That's a beautiful way to teach modernisation. It's more build, though.

So the honest recommendation: build the continuum for this, not a sort. It's the right tool for a concept that's about degree and transition. And it conveniently means that when we build it, you'll have the continuum engine ready to reuse for other spectrum topics (left/right politics, formal/informal language, etc.).

A few things to decide before I build:

You said: Q: Which version of the continuum?

Q: Which version of the continuum? A: Place items, then drop a marker for 'where society sits now'

Q: What should students place on the line? A: A mix of both

Q: How should it handle answers? (continuums usually aren't scored) A: No scoring — place &amp; discuss, share to class screen

12:52

Claude responded: That's a strong configuration — place a mix of relationships and institutions along the line, then drop a "where does society sit now" marker, no scoring, shar…

That's a strong configuration — place a mix of relationships and institutions along the line, then drop a "where does society sit now" marker, no scoring, share to screen. The society-marker is the move that turns a sorting exercise into Tönnies' actual argument: once students have placed the individual items, asking "and where's the whole society?" forces them to synthesise, and the share-to-screen lets you put three students' different society-markers side by side and ask why they disagree. That's the lesson.

Let me think through the items before building, since a mix needs to span the full line with good contestable middle:

Hard Gemeinschaft (left): a family dinner, a tight-knit rural town, a religious congregation, your oldest friendship.
Hard Gesellschaft (right): buying coffee from a stranger, a multinational corporation, an online marketplace transaction, a government tax office.
The contested middle (the gold): a long-running local footy club, a small family-run business, your relationship with a GP you've seen for years, a neighbourhood you've lived in a long time, a university, a trade union, a long-term group chat.

That spread gives clear anchors at each end so students orient themselves, then a rich middle where reasonable people place things differently. Let me build it — this is the continuum engine, which is a genuinely new interaction (placing along a line, not into buckets), so it's worth getting the mechanics right.

Created a file, ran a command, viewed a file

That's the continuum working. The line runs green (Gemeinschaft) to blue (Gesellschaft) with a subtle gradient and a dashed midline, double-headed arrow axis, the two poles labelled with their definitions, fourteen items in the bank each tagged relationship or institution, the rose "Add society now marker" button, share-to-screen, and the optional justification. The two-tone gradient does quiet pedagogical work — it visually reinforces that the line is a spectrum, not two bins.

Let me verify the placement and society marker actually work by dropping a few tiles and the marker, then sharing.

Ran a command, viewed a file

Everything works. Tiles drop and stay where placed — family dinner pinned hard left (Gemeinschaft), buying coffee far right (Gesellschaft), footy club in the contested middle, tax office on the right. The rose "SOCIETY NOW ⇄" marker appeared and stands as a vertical line you slide along the spectrum. The bank shrinks as items are placed. This is the continuum doing exactly what the concept needs — showing degree and blend, not forcing binary bins.

Presented file

There's the Gemeinschaft–Gesellschaft continuum. Open it and have a play — drag items along the line, hit "Add society now marker" and slide it, then Share to screen and open the link in a new tab to see the presented view (it loads the exact arrangement, the society marker, and the justification, clean for the projector — same mechanism as the diamond).

A few honest notes:

The share link is long (the whole arrangement is encoded in it), same trade-off as the diamond — fine since students send it to you rather than typing it.

One pedagogical caution worth holding: because there's no "correct" placement, a student could drop everything in a clump and technically "finish." The justification box and the share-to-screen accountability are what guard against that — knowing their reasoning might go on the projector pushes them to think. If you find some students gaming it, the fix is to require the justification rather than leave it optional, which I can switch on.

And the "society now" marker is the intellectual payoff — when you put three students' continua side by side on the screen, the disagreement about where society as a whole sits, and which direction it's drifting, is Tönnies' modernisation thesis turned into a live class argument. That's the moment to mine.

To get it onto the Thinkery, the usual two steps: upload continuum_gemeinschaft.html to your repo, and then I'll add a card for it on the splash page. But — given you said the Encarta splash "wasn't really it," I've held off touching the splash page further. Right now your live splash is whichever version you last uploaded. So before I add a continuum card, tell me: where did we land on the splash page look? Do you want to go back to the clean modern version (I have it backed up), keep tweaking toward the 90s feel, or something else? Once we settle the splash style, adding this card is a two-minute job.

Continuum gemeinschaft
Code · HTML
